selector.exact: the venture of predetermining history

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: Spengler's boast that the future stages of a Culture can be foretold is the single claim later scholars found least defensible. According to H. Stuart Hughes, Spengler is most valuable read not as a prophet whose dating can be checked, but as a morphologist of cultural style — the predictive apparatus is the part of the system that has worn worst.

selector.exact: Is there a logic of history?

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.anchor: Is there a logic of history?
decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: Spengler opens the whole work with this question. For a first reading, treat the Introduction as the thesis in miniature: everything that follows is an attempt to answer yes — that history has a discernible form, a 'morphology', as exact as the form of a plant. Don't get lost in the cascade of examples; hold onto the question.

selector.exact: we have the perfectly ahistoric soul

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: Spengler ranks Cultures by their relation to time, calling the Classical and Indian souls 'ahistoric' — incapable, he claims, of feeling history as the West does. Read this as a value-laden hierarchy typical of early-20th-century German thought: it is doing analytic work for his system, but it also flattens Indian and Greek thought into a foil for the 'Faustian'. Later scholarship treats the ranking as the system's bias, not its discovery.

selector.exact: the Copernican discovery in the historical sphere

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.anchor: the Copernican discovery in the historical sphere
decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: A characteristic Spenglerian gesture: just as Copernicus displaced the Earth from the centre of the cosmos, Spengler claims to displace Western Europe from the centre of history, demoting 'ancient–medieval–modern' to a parochial, Ptolemaic conceit. Watch how often he reaches for an astronomical or mathematical figure to dignify a historical claim.

selector.exact: a creation of the Magian world-sense

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: 'Magian' is Spengler's name for the Culture of the early Christian, Jewish, Byzantine and Islamic East — the soul whose prime-symbol is the world as a cavern, an enclosed space lit between heaven and earth. He treats Christianity, the Talmud, and the Qur'an as expressions of one and the same Magian springtime.

selector.exact: conceptions of decadence, militarism, the transvaluation of all

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.anchor: decadence … the transvaluation of all values
decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: Spengler names Nietzsche as one of his two masters ('Goethe gave me method, Nietzsche the questioning faculty'). 'Decadence' and 'the transvaluation of all values' are Nietzsche's terms; Spengler borrows the diagnosis of cultural exhaustion while discarding Nietzsche's hope of an overcoming. The two share a vocabulary and a mood, not a remedy.

selector.exact: “contemporary” in our special sense—with the phase of Hellenism

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: Spengler's idea of 'contemporaneity': two events in different Cultures are 'contemporary' when they occupy the same position in their respective life-cycles, regardless of calendar date. Our own modern age, he argues, stands where Hellenism stood in the Classical — both are the Culture's late, sceptical, cosmopolitan autumn. This homology is the engine of the Comparative Tables.

selector.exact: The Civilization is the inevitable destiny of the Culture

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.anchor: Civilization is the inevitable destiny of the Culture
decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: The book's central opposition. A 'Culture' (Kultur) is the living, creative spring-and-summer of a people's soul; 'Civilization' (Zivilisation) is its frozen winter — the late, megalopolitan, irreligious, money-and-machine stage after creative force is spent. Spengler insists the West has already crossed from the one into the other; the 'decline' is this crossing.

selector.exact: a morphology of world-history

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: 'Morphology' is the study of living forms and their transformations — a method Spengler takes directly from Goethe's botany and applies to history. Cultures are treated not as a chain of causes but as organisms with a shape and a life-cycle. This is the master-method of the entire book.

selector.exact: Decline of that West-European Culture

decline-vol-i-ch-01-introduction.note: 'Decline' renders the German Untergang — literally a 'going-under', the setting of the sun, a downfall. Atkinson's 'Decline' is gentler than the German; Spengler's title Der Untergang des Abendlandes carries the finality of a sunset (Abendland, the 'evening-land', is itself the German word for the West). The title is an image, not a forecast of catastrophe.

selector.exact: Das Werden, das Gewordne

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: Spengler's German pair: das Werden, 'the becoming' (living process, direction, time) and das Gewordene, 'the become' (the finished, extended, spatial thing). He takes the distinction from Goethe and builds the whole book on it — Nature is the realm of the become, History the realm of becoming. Number, he will argue, belongs wholly to the become.

selector.exact: Nature is the numerable, while History, on the other hand, is the aggregate of that which has no relation to mathematics

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: Nature is the numerable, while History … has no relation to mathematics
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: According to Northrop Frye, the framework here is a Romantic one 'derived ultimately from Fichte's adaptation of Kant': the world known to science and mathematics is the spatial, mechanical domain of Nature, set over against the time-world of life and history, which can be reached only by feeling, intuition and symbol. Read in that light, Spengler's banishing of number to the side of 'Nature' is less a thesis in the philosophy of mathematics than a move in this larger metaphysics — and, Frye adds, the analogical method it rests on can no longer be dismissed as merely 'mystical'.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: Gothic cathedrals and Doric temples are mathematics in stone.

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: Gothic cathedrals and Doric temples are mathematics in stone.
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: One of the book's most memorable images, and a good test of Spengler's method. He claims a Culture's mathematic is already present in its earliest art — the Doric column, the Gothic vault — before any mathematician states it: the Euclidean geometry 'actualized' in Classical ornament, the calculus in Gothic architecture. Whether this is a deep insight or a poet's analogy is precisely the question the chapter forces on you.

selector.exact: Pythagoras was the first in the Classical Culture to conceive number scientifically

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: Pythagoras … to conceive number scientifically … as standard and as magnitude
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: Pythagoras of Samos (6th century BC) gave his name to a school that saw number as the key to the world-order — the cosmos structured by numerical relationships, and famously the musical concords answering to simple whole-number ratios (the octave 2:1, the fifth 3:2, the fourth 4:3). This is number as magnitude and proportion, exactly the Classical conception Spengler is describing. He treats Pythagoras not as the discoverer of a universal truth but as the man through whom the Apollinian soul first found its own number.

selector.exact: There is not, and cannot be, number as such.

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: There is not, and cannot be, number as such.
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: This is the chapter's scandalous thesis, and the reason mathematics comes so early in the book. Spengler denies that there is one universal mathematics that every people slowly discovers; instead each Culture grows its own number-world out of its own soul. Don't read the chapter for math you must follow — read it for the claim that even number, the thing we treat as most certainly universal, turns out to be culturally shaped. If that holds for number, Spengler thinks, it can hold for everything.

selector.exact: the inner structure of the Euclidean geometry is something quite different from that of the Cartesian

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: the inner structure of the Euclidean geometry … quite different from … the Cartesian
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: Spengler's core mathematical claim, made concrete. Classical (Apollinian) number is magnitude — the unit, the measurable amount, embodied in Euclid's geometry of figures and the Pythagorean ratios. Western (Faustian) number is function — a pure relation, a variable, the limit and the infinite, embodied in Descartes's coordinate geometry and the Newton–Leibniz calculus. To pass from Euclid to the calculus is, for him, to pass from one soul to another.

selector.exact: the Infinitesimal Calculus in the earliest forms of Gothic architecture

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: The infinitesimal calculus — the mathematics of change, the variable and the limit — was created in the late 17th century, independently by Isaac Newton (his 'fluxions') and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (the differential and integral calculus), and made rigorous in the 19th century by Cauchy and Weierstrass. For Spengler this is the Faustian 'number as function', the soul of infinite space made calculable — and he provocatively dates its spirit to the Gothic, centuries before Newton and Leibniz were born.

selector.exact: the supposed constant structure of the intellect is an illusion

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: the supposed constant structure of the intellect is an illusion
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: The chapter's hardest and most sweeping claim. Kant had argued that the basic forms of human cognition are a fixed, universal a priori — the same for every mind. Spengler denies it: there is 'more than one style of knowing', each bound to a Culture. The stakes are enormous (if true, no knowledge is fully universal) and the claim is also self-endangering, since it seems to relativise Spengler's own system as well. Hold the difficulty; readers have split sharply ever since over whether this is profound or self-refuting.

selector.exact: The comparative morphology of knowledge forms is a domain which Western thought has still to attack.

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: The comparative morphology of knowledge forms
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: Here Spengler names his own method, and his ambition: a comparative morphology — a Goethean botany — not of plants but of the forms of knowing. Each Culture's logic and mathematics is one such form, to be compared with the others as one compares the leaf of one plant with another: by shape and stage of growth, not by which is 'true'.

selector.exact: There is no mathematic but only mathematics.

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: There is no mathematic but only mathematics.
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: A signature Spenglerian aphorism: he dissolves a supposed universal ('the mathematic') into a plurality of self-contained, culture-bound mathematics. Notice the rhetorical shape — it is the same move he made in the Introduction when he demoted 'ancient–medieval–modern' to a parochial scheme. The epigram does a great deal of persuasive work on its own; weigh whether the contrast it asserts is argued or simply declared.

selector.exact: In the first case, the agent was Pythagoras, in the second Descartes.

decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.anchor: the agent was Pythagoras … the second Descartes
decline-vol-i-ch-02-the-meaning-of-numbe.note: Spengler pairs two founding acts: Pythagoras gives the Classical soul its number (magnitude, the ratio); Descartes gives the Western soul its number (the coordinate, the function). He insists the two acts are 'at bottom the same' — each is a soul winning its own mathematic — even though their content is opposite. Pairing figures a thousand years apart by their place in the life-cycle, rather than by date, is exactly the 'contemporaneity' the Comparative Tables tabulate.

selector.exact: to view the whole fact of Man from an immense distance

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: to view the whole fact of Man from an immense distance
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler again leans on Nietzsche — whom his Preface names, beside Goethe, as one of the two to whom he owes 'practically everything' ('Goethe gave me method, Nietzsche the questioning faculty') — for the ideal of a detached, far-off view of humanity, the better to see whole Cultures as one sees peaks along a horizon. Characteristically, he adds a jab that Nietzsche himself was 'far from possessing enough of it'. The detachment Spengler wants here is the historian's version of Nietzsche's distance from his own age.

selector.exact: but the moon, too, seems to us bigger than Jupiter or Saturn.

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: the moon, too, seems to us bigger than Jupiter or Saturn
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: Watch the move: an optical illusion (the near moon looks larger than the far planets) is offered as proof of a claim about history (the recent past looks more important than the remote past). Spengler reaches, as so often, for an astronomical figure to lend a historical argument the prestige of physics. It is vivid and persuasive — and worth asking whether the analogy actually establishes the point or merely illustrates it.

selector.exact: for the Chinese historian, on the contrary, who looks back on and judges by 4000 years of Chinese history

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: for the Chinese historian … 4000 years of Chinese history
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: A pointed thought-experiment. To a Western eye, 1500–1800 in Europe is the decisive third of 'world'-history; to a Chinese historian measuring by four thousand years, those same centuries are a minor episode beside the Han dynasty. Spengler uses the reversal to expose how our periodization smuggles in our own standpoint — the very Eurocentrism the chapter sets out to break.

selector.exact: the worlds of Plato, Rembrandt, Goethe and Beethoven, the second in the worlds of Parmenides, Descartes, Kant and Newton

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: Plato, Rembrandt, Goethe and Beethoven … Parmenides, Descartes, Kant and Newton
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler sorts the great names into two camps. On the side of History (contemplation, becoming, time) stand the poets and seers — Plato, Rembrandt, Goethe, Beethoven. On the side of Nature (cognition, the become, space) stand the system-builders — Parmenides, Descartes, Kant, Newton. The rosters dramatize the book's founding distinction between the world-as-history and the world-as-nature.

selector.exact: contemplation and vision become an experience which can only be rendered in forms of art.

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: an experience which can only be rendered in forms of art
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: A claim that decides everything about how you read the book. Spengler holds that once history is grasped at its deepest — as pure becoming — it passes beyond what science can state and can be rendered only as art, like Dante's or Goethe's vision. This is why sympathetic critics have called The Decline a poem rather than a treatise; it is also why hostile ones say it cannot be checked. Hold the difficulty: it is the hinge between admiring the book and trusting it.

selector.exact: The Morphology of the organic, of history and life and all that bears the sign of direction and destiny, is called Physiognomic.

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: The Morphology of the organic … is called Physiognomic
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: Here Spengler defines his two methods outright. The Systematic dissects dead Nature into laws and causes — it is what the sciences do. The Physiognomic reads a living whole by its look and gesture, the way one reads a face or the growth of a plant; it is intuitive and analogical, and the only road into History. Comparative morphology, the method of the whole book, is physiognomic tact made rigorous.

selector.exact: Faust the portrait of a whole Culture.

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: Faust the portrait of a whole Culture.
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler likens his method to portraiture: as Rembrandt's self-portraits are an autobiography, so the lives of Cultures are to be 'painted' rather than dissected — Faust, for him, is the portrait of the whole Western soul. This is the physiognomic method declaring itself an art. Notice that it quietly licenses Spengler to value evidence by how expressive it is, not by how it bears on a proof.

selector.exact: Cultures are organisms, and world-history is their collective biography.

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: Cultures are organisms, and world-history is their collective biography.
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: If you remember one sentence from this chapter, make it this one. It is the master-image of the whole book: a Culture is not a chapter in a single human story but a living organism with a birth, youth, maturity, and death, and 'world-history' is just the comparative biography of these organisms set side by side. Everything else in the chapter — the attack on 'progress', the demand to de-centre our own age — follows from taking the metaphor literally.

selector.exact: the comparative morphology of plants and animals has long ago given us the methods

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: the comparative morphology of plants and animals has long ago given us the methods
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: The organism analogy — whole Cultures living and dying like plants — is the device critics attack most often. Northrop Frye, however, argues that there is 'no inherent improbability' in supposing that the organic rhythm of birth, growth and death which governs every individual life extends to larger human units; and that because Spengler's method is avowedly analogical, it can 'no longer be dismissed as merely mystical or irrational' on that account. Frye thus treats the biology as a working analogy to be judged by its fruits, not a literal claim to be refuted.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: hidden all too deep under the surface of a trite

decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.anchor: hidden all too deep under the surface of a trite “history of human progress”
decline-vol-i-ch-03-the-problem-of-world.note: The phrase the chapter is built to demolish: the 'history of human progress', the comfortable Victorian story in which all ages climb toward the present. Spengler dismisses it as trite and provincial — there is no single ladder of progress, only many Cultures each completing its own shape. Watch how thoroughly the anti-progress conviction shapes everything the book sees.

selector.exact: the soul is the idea of an existence

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: the soul is the idea of an existence
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: A compact statement of what Spengler means by the soul of a Culture: not a ghost inside people but the idea or possibility of an existence, working itself out as a destiny. To grasp a Culture's soul is to feel the single shape its whole life is bent on actualizing — which is why, for Spengler, soul and destiny are nearly the same thing.

selector.exact: directed, irrevocable in every line, fate-laden

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: directed, irrevocable in every line, fate-laden
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: According to Theodor Adorno, this fate-laden picture of life is exactly where Spengler must be resisted. By making a Culture's course as irrevocable as an organism's death, Spengler 'fatalizes' history and writes off human agency — turning decline into a sentence to be suffered rather than a situation to be acted in. Yet Adorno concedes the diagnostic power of Spengler's reading of the late, mechanized age, and argues against him that the very process of decay sets free forces his deterministic gaze cannot see.

selector.exact: the World-as-history as opposed to the World-as-nature

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: the World-as-history as opposed to the World-as-nature
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler ties this chapter back to his founding distinction: words like luck, doom and vocation live in the World-as-history — the world felt as becoming and destiny — not in the World-as-nature that science measures. Destiny is simply what the world-as-history feels like from inside.

selector.exact: There is an organic logic, an instinctive, dream-sure logic of all existence

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: an organic logic, an instinctive, dream-sure logic of all existence
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: The chapter's most powerful — and most slippery — move. Spengler posits a second 'logic', an 'organic' or 'dream-sure' logic of life that the ordinary logic of 'understanding' (Aristotle, Kant) simply cannot reach. It is a real insight that lived time is not like measured space; but notice that it also conveniently places Spengler's deepest claims beyond the range of logical objection. Hold the difficulty: much of whether you find the book profound or evasive turns on whether you grant this 'other logic'.

selector.exact: But destiny is the word for an inner certainty that is not describable.

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: destiny is the word for an inner certainty that is not describable
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler's master-opposition stated plainly. Causality is 'the reasonable, the law-bound, the describable' — the world the sciences can capture in number and law. Destiny is an inner certainty that cannot be reasoned out at all, only lived and felt. The whole book turns on the claim that history belongs to the second order, not the first.

selector.exact: Destiny and Causality are related as Time and Space.

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: Destiny and Causality are related as Time and Space.
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: The chapter compressed into one equation. Causality is the logic of space — of things laid out side by side, dissected into cause and effect; it is how we know Nature. Destiny is the logic of time — of one thing growing irreversibly out of another; it is how we live History. Keep the proportion in mind (Destiny : Causality :: Time : Space) and the rest of the chapter falls into place.

selector.exact: No one foreknew the storm of Islam at the coming of Mohammed, nor foresaw Napoleon in the fall of Robespierre.

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: No one foreknew … Mohammed … nor foresaw Napoleon in the fall of Robespierre
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler pairs the unforeseeable across Cultures: the Magian eruption of Islam under Mohammed, the Faustian arrival of Napoleon out of the Revolution. The surface of history, he says, is ruled by 'the unforeseen' — individual events and great men are incalculable. Hold this against his famous claim to predict the stages of a Culture: he is distinguishing the calculable rhythm of the whole life-cycle from the incalculable detail of any given hour. Whether that distinction really saves the predictive boast is a fair question to press.

selector.exact: if no one questions me, I know: if I would explain to a questioner, I know not.

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: if no one questions me, I know: if I would explain to a questioner, I know not
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler borrows St Augustine's famous remark about Time and turns it onto Destiny: both are known with perfect inward certainty until the moment one tries to explain them, when the certainty dissolves. The move is characteristic — Destiny, like lived time, is something you can be sure of but cannot define, which is precisely why (he holds) it belongs to vision and art rather than to science.

selector.exact: The Predestination doctrine of Calvin and Pascal

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: The Predestination doctrine of Calvin and Pascal
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: A revealing reading: Spengler treats the West's theology of Grace and predestination (Augustine, Calvin, Pascal) as the Destiny-idea expressed in religious form — and judges that when Calvin and Pascal tried to reason it out, they 'lost the destiny-logic' and fell into mere causal machinery. Watch how he absorbs Christian doctrine, Protestant predestination and even Darwinian 'evolution' into the single Faustian feeling for an irreversible, directed existence.

selector.exact: he who is to himself a Destiny (like Napoleon)

decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.anchor: he who is to himself a Destiny (like Napoleon)
decline-vol-i-ch-04-the-problem-of-world.note: Spengler's striking idea of the world-historical individual: a man like Napoleon does not need to 'read' history because he is himself a Destiny — between his own acts and the great facts around him there is a 'harmony of metaphysical rhythm' giving his decisions a dreamlike certainty. It is an exhilarating thought and a dangerous one; note that it makes the great actor answerable to fate rather than to reason or to law.

selector.exact: Chinese, in free hither-and-thither wandering that nevertheless goes to the goal; Egyptian in resolute march down the path once entered

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: Chinese … free … wandering … to the goal; Egyptian … resolute march down the path
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: Spengler reads a Culture's feeling for direction off its sense of life: Classical man clings to the near present, Faustian man strains toward the most distant horizon, the Chinese wanders by winding ways that still arrive, the Egyptian marches resolutely down the one path he has entered. These directional styles are the seeds of the Egyptian prime-symbol (the Way) and the Chinese (the Tao) that organize the Comparative Tables.

selector.exact: for the Classical world-view the near, strictly limited, self-contained Body, for the Western infinitely wide and infinitely profound three-dimensional Space, for the Arabian the world as a Cavern

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: the … Body … the Western … Space … the Arabian the world as a Cavern
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: Here are the three prime-symbols stated together. The Classical (Apollinian) soul takes as its root-symbol the near, bounded, self-contained Body; the Western (Faustian) soul, pure infinite three-dimensional Space; the Arabian (Magian) soul, the world as a Cavern (the domed mosque, the enclosed cosmos of light and dark). From each, Spengler says, the Culture's whole world is deduced.

selector.exact: connecting it with an early Hellenic belief

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: Spengler insists the prime-symbol is at once innate and re-enacted: every child of a Culture unfolds its depth-symbol 'as the emerging butterfly unfolds its wings', recovering what was always its own — which he aligns with Plato's anamnesis, knowledge as recollection. Note the two patrons of the move: Goethe's living form (the butterfly) and Plato's recollection, enlisted to make the prime-symbol feel discovered rather than asserted.

selector.exact: we shall consider the kind of extension as the prime symbol of a Culture

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: the kind of extension as the prime symbol of a Culture
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: This is the engine of the whole book and the hardest idea to take on first reading. Spengler claims that each Culture is born with a single root-intuition of space — a 'prime-symbol' — and that everything the Culture then makes (its mathematics, art, religion, politics) is that one feeling unfolding. Don't demand proof here; grasp the move. Once you accept that a Culture has one space-feeling, the side-by-side comparisons of the rest of the book follow.

selector.exact: the prime-symbol of the Classical soul is the material and individual body, that of the Western pure infinite space

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: the prime-symbol of the Classical soul is the … body, that of the Western … infinite space
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: Northrop Frye, expounding the system, runs through the same prime-symbols — the cavern for the Magian (the mosque), the straight way for the Egyptian, the garden for the wandering Chinese — and singles out the Western: its central symbol is 'a centre with radiating points', a 'drive into infinite distance that makes it unique among other cultures', whose central art is contrapuntal music as Classical culture's was sculpture. Frye takes the scheme seriously as imaginative morphology, the most fertile part of Spengler's vision.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: Infinite space is the ideal that the Western soul has always striven to find

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: Infinite space is the ideal that the Western soul has always striven to find
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: The Faustian prime-symbol named directly: pure, infinite space. Spengler reads the West's centuries of space-theories, its perspective painting, its long-range artillery and its calculus as so many attempts to seize the limitless — the same yearning Goethe gave to Faust. Watch how he treats even physics as a 'symptom of a world-feeling' rather than neutral knowledge.

selector.exact: the whole Classical world never expended one word on it

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: the whole Classical world never expended one word on it
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: A favourite Spenglerian argument from silence: the problem of space, which obsesses the West from Descartes to Kant to Einstein, the Classical world never even posed — it had no word for it. For Spengler this silence is not an oversight but proof that space was simply not the Greeks' prime-symbol; the bounded body was. Each Culture, on this view, can only ask the questions its own soul contains.

selector.exact: tackled with a passion that itself is symbolic

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: the “eternal problem” that Kant … tackled with a passion that itself is symbolic
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: A characteristic reversal. Kant treated the nature of space as the eternal problem of the human mind as such; Spengler answers that Kant's very passion for it is a symptom — the problem is parochially Western, unthinkable to a Classical mind. The move turns a philosopher's claim to universality into evidence for Spengler's thesis of culture-bound cognition. It is brilliant and circular at once; weigh which.

selector.exact: is a purely Western problem that simply does not arise in the intellects of other Cultures

decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.anchor: a purely Western problem that … does not arise in the intellects of other Cultures
decline-vol-i-ch-05-makrokosmos-the-symb.note: The strong relativist claim, and the chapter's deep difficulty. If the very questions a mind can ask are fixed by its Culture's prime-symbol, then Cultures are, at bottom, mutually unintelligible — a Greek could not even be made to see the West's problem of space. This gives Spengler enormous explanatory reach but at a steep price: taken strictly it would make his own cross-cultural insight impossible. Hold the tension; it is the same self-endangering relativism met in the chapter on number.

selector.exact: chose the sensuously-present individual body as the ideal type of the extended

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: the sensuously-present individual body as the ideal type of the extended
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: The Apollinian (Classical) soul defined: it takes the sensuous, present, individual body as the measure of all things. Hence its mathematics is geometry of bounded magnitudes, its art the free-standing nude statue, its physics a cosmos of bodies, its self the soma — with, Spengler claims, no feeling for inner development, distance, or history.

selector.exact: the name (familiarized by Nietzsche) of the Apollinian

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: the name (familiarized by Nietzsche) of the Apollinian
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: Spengler takes the word 'Apollinian' from Nietzsche, who in The Birth of Tragedy opposed the Apollinian (form, measure, the dream-image) to the Dionysian (intoxication, music, the dissolving of the individual). Spengler keeps the Apollinian but quietly drops the Dionysian partner, repurposing the term to name the whole Classical soul rather than one pole within it — a telling adaptation of his acknowledged master.

selector.exact: the Faustian soul, whose prime-symbol is pure and limitless space

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: the Faustian soul, whose prime-symbol is pure and limitless space
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: The Faustian (Western) soul defined: pure, limitless space, in a Culture that blossomed with the Romanesque style in the 10th century, between the Elbe and the Tagus. Its signatures are Galileian dynamics, the great dynasties, the Madonna-ideal and the destiny of Lear — and its art the space-creating ones, oil-painting and the fugue. Named for Goethe's Faust, the soul that will trade anything for the unbounded.

selector.exact: The nude statue is Apollinian, the art of the fugue Faustian.

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: The nude statue is Apollinian, the art of the fugue Faustian.
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: Northrop Frye reads this art-contrast as the clearest window onto the two souls: 'the central art of Faustian man is contrapuntal music; Classical culture expressed its sense of the pure present in its sculpture.' For Frye the Faustian is defined by 'a drive into infinite distance' — so its proper art is the one that unfolds, develops and fills time and space (the fugue), where the Classical fixes the bounded present in the standing body.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: Apollinian are: mechanical statics, the sensuous cult of the Olympian gods

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: Apollinian are: mechanical statics, the sensuous cult of the Olympian gods …
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: This is the chapter's method laid bare: Spengler draws up two long opposed lists — Apollinian on one side, Faustian on the other — sorting statics against dynamics, the city-state against the dynastic state, Œdipus against Lear. Read the lists for the family-resemblance he is after, but keep a critical eye: a scheme that can file every phenomenon under one of two souls is powerful precisely because almost nothing can refute it.

selector.exact: the Magian soul of the Arabian Culture with its algebra, astrology and alchemy

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: the Magian soul of the Arabian Culture with its algebra, astrology and alchemy
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: The Magian (Arabian) soul defined: the Culture that wakes around the time of Augustus in the lands between Nile and Tigris, expressing itself in algebra, astrology and alchemy, in mosaic and arabesque, in caliphate and mosque, and in the scriptures of the Persian, Jewish, Christian and Manichæan religions alike. Its prime-symbol is the world-cavern — the enclosed, vaulted space of spirit and matter.

selector.exact: could not be represented in an Apollinian language, whether Greek or Latin

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: could not be represented in an Apollinian language, whether Greek or Latin
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: The same hard relativism that runs through the book, now applied to the arts and languages: Faustian space, Spengler says, simply could not be uttered in Greek or Latin, and the Apollinian's bounded space is equally closed to us. The claim gives the soul-types their depth — but pressed strictly it would make translation, and Spengler's own reading of the Greeks, impossible. Hold the difficulty rather than resolve it.

selector.exact: The Doric column bores into the ground

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: The Doric column bores into the ground
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: A vivid morphological reading of architecture. The Doric column 'bores into the ground' and the temple emphasizes its footing — the Apollinian soul wants the firm, bounded, standing body. Against it Spengler sets the Gothic nave soaring up and the Magian cupola closing over: three souls, three ways of meeting space — to stand on it, to soar into it, to be roofed within it.

selector.exact: Siegfried, Parzeval, Tristan, Hamlet, Faust are the loneliest heroes in all the Cultures.

decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.anchor: Siegfried, Parzeval, Tristan, Hamlet, Faust are the loneliest heroes in all the Cultures.
decline-vol-i-ch-06-makrokosmos-apollini.note: One of the chapter's most beautiful passages, and a good example of Spengler arguing by resonance rather than proof. The infinite space of the Faustian soul is felt, he says, as infinite solitude — so its heroes (Siegfried, Parzeval, Tristan, Hamlet, Faust) are the loneliest in any Culture, while Olympus sits on homely Greek soil. Persuasive and moving; ask whether it demonstrates the thesis or simply illustrates a mood.

selector.exact: with these arts we count music in its many and very dissimilar kinds

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: with these arts we count music in its many and very dissimilar kinds
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: The move that organizes the whole chapter: Spengler refuses to file music apart from the 'pictorial-plastic' arts. The division that matters, he says, is not eye versus ear (a 'superficial' physiological distinction) but the inner form-language of a Culture. So a Rembrandt and a Bach fugue can be nearer kin than two paintings. Read the chapter as an argument that all of a Culture's arts say one thing.

selector.exact: the space-straining music since Bach

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: Northrop Frye endorses exactly this ranking of music: 'the central art of Faustian man is contrapuntal music', as 'Classical culture expressed its sense of the pure present in its sculpture.' For Frye the Faustian is the culture of 'a drive into infinite distance', so its supreme art is the one that builds and develops in time and space — the fugue and symphony — which is why Spengler's 'space-straining music' is, on this reading, the West's most revealing art.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: the truly Faustian and wholly un-Classical power of imagination

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: the truly Faustian and wholly un-Classical power of imagination
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: A defining Faustian trait: art that appeals past the bodily senses to an 'inner' eye and ear — the imagination. We read Faust and study orchestral scores in silence, letting the inner sense supply what the outer cannot. Spengler ties this to the Faustian feeling for infinite space: the work points beyond any sensuous surface to 'a whole world of others' behind it.

selector.exact: Shakespeare’s ceaseless change of scene as against the Classical unity of place

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: Shakespeare’s ceaseless change of scene as against the Classical unity of place
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: A neat illustration of the two world-feelings in dramatic form. Classical tragedy keeps the unity of place — one bounded here-and-now, like the standing body; Shakespeare ranges freely across space and time, addressing the inner imagination. The 'unity of place' is Apollinian; the restless scene-change is Faustian. The same opposition Spengler finds between statue and fugue, he finds between Sophocles and Shakespeare.

selector.exact: It is not an incident that Beethoven wrote his last works when he was deaf

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: It is not an incident that Beethoven wrote his last works when he was deaf
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: A characteristic Spenglerian flourish, and a good one to weigh. Beethoven's deafness, he says, was no accident but a release — 'sight and hearing equally are bridges into the soul and nothing more.' It is a thrilling claim about the inwardness of Faustian art; notice that it also converts a biographical fact into a symbol with a confidence that outruns any evidence.

selector.exact: He felt the marble with his eye

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: He felt the marble with his eye
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: The Apollinian counter-case: the Greek's enjoyment of art was sensuous and bodily — he 'felt the marble with his eye', and the aulos moved him almost physically; eye and ear received the whole of the work. The Faustian appeal to an inner, visionary imagination was, Spengler says, simply alien to him. The contrast is the chapter's engine: two utterly different relations between the art-work and the senses.

selector.exact: that of Rembrandt as that of the organ works of Buxtehude, Pachelbel and Bach

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: Rembrandt … the organ works of Buxtehude, Pachelbel and Bach
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: Spengler's most concrete statement of the thesis: within one Culture-period, a painting and a piece of music share an 'inner form-language' so nearly identical that the optical/acoustic difference is negligible. Poussin's landscape answers to the chamber-cantata, Rembrandt to Bach's organ works, Guardi to Mozart's operas. The pairings are meant as morphological equations, not mere analogies.

selector.exact: the difference between optical and acoustic means is negligible

decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.anchor: the difference between optical and acoustic means is negligible
decline-vol-i-ch-07-music-and-plastic-th.note: The chapter's boldest methodological claim, and a real difficulty. To say the sensory medium of an art is 'negligible' beside its inner form-language lets Spengler equate a painting with a fugue — a liberating idea for cultural analysis, but one that quietly discards exactly what painters and composers labour over. Hold the question: how much of an art really is its medium? Spengler's whole comparative method needs the answer to be 'little'.

selector.exact: long after the plastic art itself had become entirely alien to our inner soul

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: long after the plastic art itself had become entirely alien to our inner soul
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: A claim that quietly reframes how you should read the whole Western tradition. Spengler argues that the Renaissance's reverence for Classical sculpture made us misjudge our own art — we kept ranking the nude statue highest 'long after' plastic had gone dead for the Faustian soul. The difficulty: this lets him treat the entire Renaissance valuation of Classical art as a kind of collective error. Powerful, but it assumes exactly the soul-typology it is meant to illustrate.

selector.exact: found the most genuine, the only exhaustive, expression of his life-feeling in the Portrait

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: Faustian man … found … expression of his life-feeling in the Portrait
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: The core contrast. Classical man exhausts the human being in the bounding surfaces of the nude statue — the soma, the present body. Faustian man finds his fullest expression in the Portrait, which reaches behind the surface to a biography and a soul. Spengler calls the Hellenic nude 'the great exceptional case'; for him the portrait, not the statue, is the natural human image.

selector.exact: it is in the conflict of these two form-ideals that the contrast of two worlds is first manifested

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: the conflict of these two form-ideals … the contrast of two worlds
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: The chapter's organizing opposition: the Act (the nude, the deed-in-the-body) versus the Portrait. The first shows an existence in its outward, bounded structure — the Apollinian way; the second makes the inner soul speak — the Faustian way. Hold this pair (Act / Portrait) and you have the whole chapter: two arts, two souls, two ways of being a self.

selector.exact: In China the face is like a landscape

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: In China the face is like a landscape
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: A four-Culture comparison of the human face in art. The Magian mosque 'had no face' (hence Islamic iconoclasm drove the portrait out into arabesque); in Egypt the statue's face was a pylon, a mighty emergence from the stone-mass; in China the face is read 'like a landscape', full of little signs; for the West it is musical. Each Culture meets the face as its prime-symbol dictates.

selector.exact: But, for us, the portrait is musical.

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: But, for us, the portrait is musical.
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: The chapter's most beautiful equation, tying this chapter to the last: the Western portrait — 'the look, the play of the mouth, the pose of head and hands' — is 'a fugue of the subtlest meaning, a composition of many voices'. Painting and music meet again. Notice how the claim turns on the prior assumption that all Faustian arts share one inner language; granted that, the portrait simply is music in another medium.

selector.exact: as a centre of the World-as-History

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: the portrait … takes its man … as a centre of the World-as-History
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: The portrait, Spengler says, takes its subject not as a centre of the World-as-Nature (as the statue does) but as a centre of the World-as-History — a being with a past and a future, woken 'out of the stone from about 1200' and 'become completely music in the 17th Century'. The art-contrast of this chapter is the same world-as-history / world-as-nature distinction that opened the book, now read off paint and marble.

selector.exact: The Classical statue is a piece of present

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: The Classical statue is a piece of present “Nature” and nothing besides
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: A compact restatement of the Apollinian soul through its art: the Classical statue is 'a piece of present Nature and nothing besides' — it belongs wholly to the world-as-nature, the bounded body in the present, with no inner history. 'Classical poetry is statuary in verse.' This is why, for Spengler, the Greek lives 'point-wise, ahistorically, somatically' and never produced genuine self-criticism or self-portrait.

selector.exact: a self-portrait is a historical confession

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: a self-portrait is a historical confession
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: One of the chapter's richest threads: Spengler ties the Western portrait to the sacrament of Confession. A self-portrait by Rembrandt, like a Beethoven quartet or a Goethe autobiography, is a laying-bare of the inner history of a self before a judge — and even the Protestant or freethinker who refuses the priest, he says, still confesses, to himself or to all the world. The Faustian portrait is the picture-form of the confessing, historical soul.

selector.exact: So are the portraits of Rembrandt and the music of Beethoven

decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.anchor: the portraits of Rembrandt and the music of Beethoven
decline-vol-i-ch-08-music-and-plastic-ac.note: It is analyses like this one — reading a whole culture-soul off the style of its art — that H. Stuart Hughes judged the durable core of Spengler's achievement, as against the discredited prophecy of dates. Hughes reads Spengler less as a forecaster than as a morphologist of cultural style; the Act/Portrait contrast, drawing Rembrandt's portraits and Beethoven's music into one Faustian confession, is that morphology at its most suggestive — and least falsifiable.

selector.exact: always been the shallowest and most worthless of the disciplines of philosophy

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: the shallowest and most worthless of the disciplines of philosophy
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: The chapter opens by demolishing 'scientific' psychology: it fails, Spengler says, because the soul is not a systematic object that reason can dissect into elements and functions. What psychology really produces is a 'soul-image' — a picture the soul makes of itself, always coloured by the Culture doing the picturing. Read the chapter as a morphology of these soul-images, not as psychology in the laboratory sense.

selector.exact: Its searches and solutions are fights with shadows and ghosts

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Its searches and solutions are fights with shadows and ghosts
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: A sweeping dismissal worth pausing on. Spengler writes off the whole of experimental psychology as 'fights with shadows and ghosts' — a science with no real object. There is a serious point underneath (introspective categories may indeed be culture-bound), but the verdict is total, and it conveniently clears the ground for his own intuitive 'morphology of the soul'. Weigh how much is insight and how much is a rhetorical scorched-earth.

selector.exact: a name, a prime-word like God

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: Watch the move, which echoes the chapter on Destiny. 'Will', Spengler says, is no concept the psychologist can define but a 'prime-word like God' — something known with immediate inward certainty and never describable. It is the same gesture by which he placed Destiny beyond Causality: the deepest realities are felt, not analysed. For the Faustian soul, Will is the prime-word; that is why its soul-image is all direction and depth.

selector.exact: A soul-image is never anything but the image of one quite definite soul

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: A soul-image is never anything but the image of one quite definite soul
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: Spengler's key term here: the 'soul-image' (Seelenbild) is the picture a Culture forms of its own inner life — its model of what a self is. It is never neutral or universal; it is 'the image of one quite definite soul', the soul of that Culture. So every system of psychology is really a self-portrait of its age, and the soul-images of the Cultures differ as much as their mathematics or their art.

selector.exact: the soul-image underlying the knowledge is really one’s own soul-image

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: the soul-image underlying the knowledge is really one’s own soul-image
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: Spengler's claim that all psychology is secretly a Culture's self-portrait is, on H. Stuart Hughes's reading, a prime example of what survives in Spengler — the morphologist's eye for how a whole age expresses itself in its forms of thought, as against the dated forecasting Hughes judged worthless. Hughes treats this culture-bound 'soul-image' as a genuine, if overstated, contribution to the history of ideas rather than as science.

selector.exact: Plato’s biga-team

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: the Apollinian soul-image — Plato’s biga-team with νοῦς as charioteer
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: Each Culture pictures the soul in its own form. The Apollinian soul-image is Plato's chariot-team (biga) with Reason as charioteer — a small society of bodily parts in the visible present, exactly as the Classical world is a sum of bounded bodies. Set this beside the Faustian soul-image of a single deep will straining into infinity, and the difference of souls is laid bare in the very diagrams they draw of themselves.

selector.exact: Magian soul-image is a strict dualism of two mysterious substances, Spirit and Soul

decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: the Magian soul-image is a strict dualism … Spirit and Soul
decline-vol-i-ch-09-soul-image-and-life-.note: The Magian soul-image is a dualism of two substances — Spirit and Soul, light and dark, the divine breath and the lower self — locked together in the world-cavern. This is the inner shape of the Magian world: not the bounded body of the Classical nor the lonely will of the Faustian, but a battleground of two powers, the same dualism that runs through Persian, Gnostic, Christian and Islamic theology.

selector.exact: Buddha, for instance, gives a pattern to take or to leave, and Epicurus offers counsel.

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Buddha … gives a pattern to take or to leave, and Epicurus offers counsel.
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: The contrast that sets up the whole chapter. Western morale commands — 'thou shalt' — and claims universal validity; Buddha merely offers a pattern to take or leave, Epicurus a counsel, neither containing the 'will-element'. The difference is not of content but of dynamic: the Faustian ethic is a will to affect the distant, the Indian and Classical ethics are not. Same human seriousness, opposite relation to other people.

selector.exact: Even Nietzsche, that most passionate opponent of

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Even Nietzsche, that most passionate opponent of “herd morale” …
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: A sharp and characteristic stroke against his own master. Nietzsche, the great enemy of 'herd morale', is on Spengler's analysis the arch-Socialist: he could not limit his zeal to himself in the Classical way but 'thought only of mankind' and attacked all who differed — the very 'will to transform mankind' that defines ethical Socialism. The point is acute; note too how neatly it lets Spengler fold even his anti-systematic hero into the system.

selector.exact: All world-improvers are Socialists. And consequently there are no Classical world-improvers.

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: All world-improvers are Socialists … no Classical world-improvers.
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: The chapter's provocation, and its key to read by. Spengler uses 'Socialism' in an ethical, not economic, sense: the will to impose one's values on all mankind, the 'thou shalt' that wants to remake the world. By that measure the whole modern West — Luther, Nietzsche, the Jesuits, the Darwinians — is Socialist, while the Classical world, content with a 'pantheon of morales', produced no world-improvers at all. Hold this wide sense of the word or the chapter will mislead you.

selector.exact: the Buddhist ideal of Nirvana, which as a formula

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: the Buddhist ideal of Nirvana … the Katharsis … Ataraxia
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: The deepest of the parallels. Spengler aligns the Attic tragedy's Katharsis, the Stoic and Epicurean Ataraxia, and the Buddhist Nirvana as kindred end-state ideals — the purging of the will, a 'statuesque steadiness and will-less ethos'. Classical man and Indian man stand close here, and both far from the Faustian, whose tragedy is one of dynamic striving (Shakespeare), not of will-less repose. 'There is nothing preposterous in the idea of Socrates, Epicurus and Diogenes sitting by the Ganges.'

selector.exact: are morphologically equivalent as end-phenomena

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Buddhism, Stoicism and Socialism are morphologically equivalent as end-phenomena
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: This equation — Buddhism, Stoicism and Socialism as the 'contemporary' winter-ethics of three Cultures — is Spengler's comparative method at full stretch. Northrop Frye judged exactly this morphological view of history 'an immense improvement on the ordinary linear one', holding that on this point 'Spengler seems to me to be on very solid ground', since the linear assumption that the present age is the goal of all history is 'a vulgar and complacent' one whose demise Spengler helped to bring about.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler (Daedalus)

selector.exact: Socialism is the dynamic treatment of the same theme

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Socialism is the dynamic treatment of the same theme
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: Spengler's precise distinction between the two winter-ethics. Stoicism is static — 'individual self-management, statuesque and purely present being', defending the pose; Socialism is dynamic — defending not the pose but the working-out of a life, and 'with a powerful thrust into distance' it spreads over all future and all mankind. Same end-phase theme (resignation in a spent Culture), one in the Apollinian key of the present, one in the Faustian key of distance.

selector.exact: Faustian man has nothing more to hope for in anything pertaining to the grand style of Life

decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.anchor: Faustian man has nothing more to hope for in anything pertaining to the grand style of Life
decline-vol-i-ch-10-soul-image-and-life-.note: The chapter's bleakest claim, and the heart of the 'decline'. From Rousseau onward, Spengler says, 'something has come to an end': the Western soul, which 'was Will and nothing but Will', has exhausted its great possibilities and now produces only the will to create without the content — Nietzsche's Superman 'a castle in the air', the 'lie of life' (Ibsen) running through all modern art and politics. Sit with how much this verdict is diagnosis and how much is a mood raised to a law.

selector.exact: it may have an exactly-definable end

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: as a science … it may have an exactly-definable end
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: Spengler holds that Western physics, being the unfolding of one Culture's soul, is finite — it 'may have an exactly-definable end', a point at which it has said all it can say. According to John Farrenkopf, whose reassessment of Spengler gives sustained attention to his ideas on science and technology, this diagnosis of a self-limiting, self-exhausting Western science belongs to the genuinely serious side of Spengler's thought — a prescient strand of his critique of modernity, to be weighed rather than dismissed with the dated political prophecies.

attribution.work: Prophet of Decline: Spengler on World History and Politics

selector.exact: every one of its results is a symbol

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: every one of its results is a symbol
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: The book's boldest application of its method, saved for last: even physics — the hardest, most 'objective' science — is for Spengler an expression of a Culture, and 'every one of its results is a symbol'. Modern physics works because it lets us handle Nature 'as a machine', but its concepts (force, cause, motion) are Faustian myths, not neutral truths. Read the chapter as the claim that there is no culture-free knowledge of Nature, only Faustian, Apollinian and Magian natures.

selector.exact: faith is the older and it dominates all the conditions of knowing

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: faith is the older and it dominates all the conditions of knowing
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: The chapter's deepest and most contestable claim: 'Faith and knowledge are only two species of inner certitude', and faith is the elder that 'dominates all the conditions of knowing'. Even the most exact investigator, Spengler says, is 'controlled by the unconscious form' of his Culture. There is a real insight here (no inquiry is presuppositionless), but pressed to the limit it dissolves the distinction between science and myth — and, once more, would swallow Spengler's own claims. Hold the difficulty.

selector.exact: it is theories and not pure numbers that are the support of all natural science

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: it is theories and not pure numbers that are the support of all natural science
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: A genuinely sharp point about science, dressed in Spengler's metaphysics. A bare formula (s = ½gt²) is 'dumb'; it means nothing until vision (θεωρία = 'image, vision') clothes the signs in words and pictures. So 'it is theories and not pure numbers' that carry science — measurement alone is 'a delight for little minds'. Modern philosophy of science would partly agree (observation is theory-laden); notice how Spengler pushes it to the stronger claim that theory is myth.

selector.exact: a dynamic of unlimited span, a physics of the distant

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: a dynamic of unlimited span, a physics of the distant
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: Three souls, three natural sciences. Out of the Classical nude statue grew 'a static of bodies, a physics of the near' (matter and form); out of the Magian cavern-world grew Alchemy (substances with secret attributes, a means of Grace); out of the Faustian feeling for infinite space grew 'a dynamic of unlimited span, a physics of the distant' — the idea of force and mass. Each Culture's physics is its prime-symbol turned into law.

selector.exact: to the Faustian the idea of force and mass

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: to the Faustian the idea of force and mass
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: Spengler's reading of the master-concept of Western physics: 'force' is not a neutral discovery but the Faustian soul's own signature — the Will read into Nature, the demand to master action-at-a-distance. Where the Greek has 'matter and form' and the Arabian 'substances with secret attributes', the Faustian has 'force and mass'. The same will-to-the-distant that built the Gothic and the calculus also built Galilean dynamics.

selector.exact: The Greek asked, what is the essence of visible being?

decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.anchor: The Greek asked, what is the essence of visible being? We ask … mastering the invisible motive-forces
decline-vol-i-ch-11-faustian-and-apollin.note: The two sciences in two questions. The Greek asks 'what is the essence of visible being?' — and rests in contented contemplation of the present body. We ask 'what possibility is there of mastering the invisible motive-forces of becoming?' — and drive at masterful experiment and control. Apollinian theory is 'quiet meditation'; Faustian science is 'from the very outset a working hypothesis'. Pure knowing versus the will to power over Nature.

selector.exact: Regard the flowers at eventide as, one after the other, they close in the setting sun.

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Notice how Volume Two opens: not with evidence but with a prose-poem — flowers closing at dusk, the one free gnat still dancing in the light. According to Northrop Frye, this is how the whole work should be read: less as social science than as a feat of imaginative vision, 'one of the world's great Romantic poems.' Frye's point is not dismissal but redirection — judge Spengler the way you judge a poet's master-image, for its imaginative reach and coherence rather than its testability.

attribution.work: “The Decline of the West” by Oswald Spengler

selector.exact: the differentia by which we distinguish vegetable and animal existence

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Servitude and freedom … the differentia by which we distinguish vegetable and animal existence
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Orientation for the whole second volume. Vol I asked 'what is a Culture?' (Form and Actuality); Vol II asks how Cultures stand to one another in world-history (Perspectives) — and it begins, surprisingly, not with history but with biology and metaphysics. The plant (bound, 'cosmic') against the animal (free, a 'microcosm') is the bedrock on which everything later — peoples, cities, money, the State — is built. Don't skip this chapter as preamble; it is the foundation.

selector.exact: The plant is something cosmic, and the animal is additionally a microcosm in relation to a macrocosm.

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: The plant is something cosmic, and the animal is additionally a microcosm
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler's foundational pair. 'The Cosmic' is life bound into the great periodic flow of nature — the plant, rooted, moved by wind and sun, never choosing. The 'microcosm' is the free-moving animal that has detached a little world of its own and can define its position over against the All. All higher life, man included, is a microcosm that never wholly escapes its cosmic ground — a tension the chapter never lets go of.

selector.exact: All that is cosmic bears the hall-mark of periodicity

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: All that is cosmic bears the hall-mark of periodicity … microcosmic possesses … tension
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: The two rhythms of life. The cosmic side has 'beat' — periodicity, the pulse of blood and the cycles of the stars, everything Spengler files under direction, time, destiny. The microcosmic side has 'tension' — the polarity of subject and object, I and thou, cause and effect, the wakeful strain of attention. Sleep dissolves the tension and leaves only the beat ('a human being asleep … is leading only a plantlike existence'). Hold this pairing; it is the source of his Time/Space, destiny/causality oppositions.

selector.exact: The blood is for us the symbol of the living.

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: The blood is for us the symbol of the living.
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Watch the word 'blood' — it will carry enormous weight later in this volume. Here it is defined: the ceaseless cosmic stream that 'binds the generations in a great linkage of destiny, beat, and time.' Blood is Being made flesh. When Spengler reaches the modern crisis he stages it as a duel of 'blood and money'; the blood that wins the last battle (ch. XIV) is this — the cosmic, race-deep, destiny-bearing current, set against the abstract waking-being of money and intellect.

selector.exact: Being possesses beat and direction, while waking-consciousness is tension and extension.

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Being possesses beat and direction, while waking-consciousness is tension and extension.
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler's master-distinction in Vol II: 'being' (Dasein, 'being-there') versus 'waking-being' (Wachsein, waking-consciousness). Being is the deep cosmic existence — blood, destiny, the 'when and wherefore'; waking-being is the alert, sensing, knowing surface — the eye, causality, the 'where and how.' Cultures, peoples, classes are forms of Being; thought and theory belong to waking-being. When he later opposes blood to money, or the man of fact to the man of truth, this is the root.

selector.exact: our concepts are derived from vision, and the whole fabric of our logic is a light-world in the imagination

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: our concepts are derived from vision … our logic is a light-world in the imagination
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler derives all human thought from the tyranny of one sense, the eye: 'I' is a 'light-concept,' the world becomes a light-world, and even our most abstract words keep a faint 'light-value.' This is the same intuition as Vol I's Faustian prime-symbol of infinite space — the will to see into the distance. It also sets up his claim that music, alone among the arts, works outside the light-world and so can break its 'steely tyranny.'

selector.exact: It is one of the greatest achievements of Nietzsche that he confronted science with the problem of the value of truth and knowledge

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Nietzsche … confronted science with the problem of the value of truth and knowledge
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler's epistemology is Nietzschean: 'truth' is not life's master but its instrument — 'for life they are valuable or valueless.' He credits Nietzsche with forcing science to ask what truth is worth, and twits Descartes for doubting everything 'but certainly not the value of his doubting.' This is the ground for his recurring put-down of the philosopher and the 'ink-slinger' against the man of action.

selector.exact: There are born destiny-men and causality-men.

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: There are born destiny-men and causality-men.
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: The same two-types contrast that runs through Man and Technics (fact-men vs. truth-men) here gets its metaphysical name: the 'destiny-man' (peasant, warrior, statesman, entrepreneur — whole, planted, living from the blood) and the 'causality-man' (priest, savant, ideologue — living from detached understanding). Spengler's sympathy is unmistakable: 'the man of the active category is a whole man.' He will insist a Plato or Rousseau was 'without importance' for a Caesar or Napoleon — the thinker can discuss destiny; these men were destiny.

selector.exact: the higher Cultures, which are born in great spiritual upheavals

decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the higher Cultures, which are born in great spiritual upheavals … weld all aggregates … into one unit
decline-vol-ii-ch-01-origin-and-landscap.note: The chapter ends by scaling the cosmic/microcosmic pair up to history. 'Mass-souls' — peoples, armies, classes, the crowd that forms in a street and dissolves again — are cosmic unities, felt and lived, not reasoned; the greatest of them are the high Cultures themselves, which 'weld all aggregates of lower degree … into one unit.' This is Spengler's bridge from metaphysics to the world-history that fills the rest of the volume, and his definition of the statesman: the man with an eye for these mass-souls.

selector.exact: The two pictures I have called world-as-history and world-as-nature.

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: Two ways the same waking mind can take the world. The 'world-as-nature' is the picture science draws — timeless, causal, systematic, repeatable. The 'world-as-history' is the picture the living eye draws — directional, once-only, destiny-laden. For Spengler these are not two subjects but two modes of seeing, and his whole method is a wager that history needs the second, physiognomic, mode — not the borrowed causality of the first.

selector.exact: There is no history-in-itself.

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: A striking statement of Spengler's historical perspectivism: 'every man, class, nation, or family sees the picture of history in relation to itself.' The German and the Englishman see the World War differently; Talleyrand could read his own contemporaries but would have been lost among Caesar's. Each Culture even has its own characteristic horizon. This is why Spengler thinks an 'objective' universal history is an illusion — and why he is candid that his own picture is the one 'his time demands.'

selector.exact: human history, for all its immense and still unknown span, a mere episode in world-history

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: human history … a mere episode in world-history
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: The 'Copernican' move of Vol I, extended. The Faustian 'infinity-craving' first split human history from world-history, demoting man's whole career to 'a mere episode' and the Earth to 'a little star amongst millions of solar systems.' The same gesture dissolves the parochial Western scheme of 'ancient–mediæval–modern,' which Spengler says is 'visibly dissolving' — to be replaced by the eight-Culture comparative picture this volume draws.

selector.exact: There is no more conclusive refutation of Darwinism

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: There is no more conclusive refutation of Darwinism … than that furnished by palæontology
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: Read critically. Spengler claims the fossil record 'refutes' Darwin: species appear suddenly, persist unchanged, then vanish, with no gradual 'transitional' chain — so (he argues) forms unfold by an inner 'destiny' and 'form-fulfilment' (Goethe's idea), not by utilitarian selection (Darwin's). He frames it as destiny vs. causality, 'German' vs. 'English' thought. The observation about stasis in the record is real and was later named (punctuated equilibrium), but mainstream biology treats it as a pattern within evolution, not a refutation of it. Take this as Spengler's polemical recruiting of science for his morphology, not as settled fact.

selector.exact: the Chinese imperialism of Shi-hwang-ti and the Roman of Augustus

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the Chinese imperialism of Shi-hwang-ti and the Roman of Augustus, as Hellenism and Buddhism and Islam
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler's comparative tables in motion. Each Culture, he holds, passes through the same epochs in the same order, so that figures and events in different Cultures are 'contemporary' — the Chinese 'First Emperor' (Qin Shi Huang, whom Spengler casts as the 'Chinese Augustus') stands where Augustus stands in the Classical, where the coming Caesarism stands in the West. This chapter pairs Chinese, Egyptian, Classical, Mexican and Western dates relentlessly; it is the engine behind the portal's Comparative Tables.

selector.exact: the fact that we have before us eight such Cultures, all of the same build, the same development, and the same duration

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: eight such Cultures, all of the same build, the same development, and the same duration
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: The eight high Cultures are the units of Spengler's comparative morphology: Egyptian, Babylonian, Indian, Chinese, Classical (Apollinian), Arabian (Magian), Mexican, and Western (Faustian). Each is an organism with the 'same build' and life-span (~1,000 years of Culture, then Civilization). Because they are 'comparable,' he can read one against another and so 'extend our knowledge backwards over lost periods and forwards over the future' — the licence for the whole predictive apparatus.

selector.exact: this is the one example of a Culture ended by violent death

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the one example of a Culture ended by violent death
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: The Mexican Culture is Spengler's proof-case that the relations between Cultures are 'accidental.' Aztec-Maya civilization was not exhausted from within but 'murdered in the full glory of its unfolding … washed out by a handful of bandits' (Cortés) — so completely that 'the relics of the population retained not even a memory of it all.' From this he draws his bleakest thesis: 'the history of humanity has no meaning whatever,' and 'deep significances reside only in the life-courses of the separate Cultures.'

selector.exact: the history of humanity has no meaning whatever

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the history of humanity has no meaning whatever
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: This is the fatalism that Spengler's critics single out. According to Theodor Adorno, Spengler's verdicts on the doomed late Civilization carry real diagnostic force — he saw the coming megalopolis, the rule of money and technique, more clearly than his optimistic contemporaries. But the fatalism, the treatment of decline as a sealed, meaningless destiny that no will can touch, is exactly what Adorno argued must be resisted: much of what Spengler presents as 'nature' is in fact the made world of human domination, and therefore not fixed.

selector.exact: man is not only historyless before the birth of the Culture, but again becomes so

decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.anchor: man is … historyless before the birth of the Culture, but again becomes so …
decline-vol-ii-ch-02-origin-and-landscap.note: Difficult material, and a key Spenglerian ranking. He calls peoples outside a living Culture's arc — both before its birth and after a Civilization has spent itself — 'historyless,' their wars 'merely a drama of living Nature,' a 'zoölogical up-and-down.' This is the seed of his later category of the 'fellaheen' (ch. VI). Note the value-laden hierarchy: it sorts whole peoples into history-bearing and history-less, which does analytic work for his system but also flattens the lives it dismisses. Read it as the system's bias, not a neutral finding.

selector.exact: but creators that absorb. Being has been confused with waking-being

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: It is not products that 'influence,' but creators that absorb.
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: The methodological heart of the chapter, and a direct attack on the way history is usually written. 'Influence,' 'continuity,' 'permanent effects' — the causal chains beloved of the nineteenth century — are, for Spengler, illusions. Cultures do not transmit forms to one another like billiard balls; a young Culture reaches into the dead form-stock of an older one and takes only the few things its own soul could already have invented. The creator absorbs; the product does not act. This is what makes each Culture a sealed monad and licenses treating them as separate organisms.

selector.exact: Connotations are not transferable.

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: The sealed-monad thesis at its sharpest. Indian and Chinese Buddhists used the same words, rites, and symbols, yet were 'spiritually as far apart as ever' — 'two different souls, each going its own way.' Spengler insists that meaning never crosses the gulf between Cultures intact; the same scripture, the same Aristotle, the same Christ is silently re-made by each soul that receives it. Bear this in mind as a working assumption of his whole comparative method — and as one of its most disputable premises.

selector.exact: Every relation that was accepted was not only an exception, but also a misunderstanding
