decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Every relation that was accepted was … a misunderstanding
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: A wonderfully counter-intuitive idea: borrowing is creative misreading. The Renaissance did not absorb antiquity but 'misunderstood' it into something Faustian; 'the inner force of a Being is never so clearly evidenced as it is in this art of deliberate misunderstanding.' For Spengler, the more reverently a Culture praises an alien thought, 'the more fundamentally in truth we have denatured it.' What looks like fidelity to the past is really the strength of a present will-to-form.

selector.exact: Greek, Arabian, and Gothic—who had not one concept or thought in common

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the 'three Aristotles' — Greek, Arabian, and Gothic — who had not one concept or thought in common
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler's favourite example of the sealed monad. One author, Aristotle, became three incommensurable philosophies — the Greek Aristotle of the Polis, the Magian Aristotle of the Arabian commentators, and the Gothic Aristotle of the Schoolmen — 'who had not one concept or thought in common.' Likewise 'the' Christian religion, he argues, was a different thing for Magian and for Faustian man. The continuity is in the name only; the meaning is re-created each time.

selector.exact: passed through three whole courses of evolution in three Cultures, with completely different meanings in each

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Roman law … passed through three whole courses of evolution in three Cultures
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: Spengler turns to law to clinch the argument, because law looks like the most universal and transmissible thing of all. His claim: Roman law only appears to have travelled unchanged for two millennia; in fact it lived three separate lives — Classical, Magian (the Eastern Roman / Arabian jurists), and Faustian — meaning something different in each. The next notes trace the deepest of these contrasts, between the Classical and the Western legal soul.

selector.exact: the theft of electric power

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: the theft of electric power … had to be dealt with under an ad hoc statute
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: A concrete, almost prophetic illustration. Around 1900 Western law could not comfortably handle the theft of electric power, patents, copyright, or 'intellectual property' — because these are not 'things' (bodies) but forces, functions, and potentialities. Spengler reads this strain as proof that the Faustian soul has outgrown the Roman 'law of things' and demands a law of forces. It is a striking moment where his morphology touches problems we still litigate.

selector.exact: Classical law was a law of bodies, while ours is a law of functions.

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: Classical law was a law of bodies, while ours is a law of functions.
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: The morphology of law in one sentence, and a perfect echo of Spengler's prime-symbols. Classical (Apollinian) law is a 'juristic statics' of bodies present-at-hand: the 'persona' is a body in the Polis, the slave a 'thing,' law made case-by-case for the moment. Western (Faustian) law is a 'juristic dynamics' — 'persons are not bodies, but units of force and will' — striving toward a complete code that decides 'every conceivable future problem.' Classical law bears 'the stamp of the moment,' Western law 'the stamp of the future.' Statics vs. dynamics, body vs. force: the same opposition that divides the Doric temple from the Gothic cathedral.

selector.exact: The Romans created a juristic statics; our task is juristic dynamics.

decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.anchor: The Romans created a juristic statics; our task is juristic dynamics.
decline-vol-ii-ch-03-origin-and-landscap.note: It is demonstrations like this one — showing that even law, seemingly the most universal of institutions, bears the physiognomic stamp of its Culture — that scholars have found the durable part of Spengler. According to H. Stuart Hughes, Spengler's lasting value lies not in his predictions or his system — whose method Hughes criticized at length — but exactly in this comparative morphology of cultural style, the trained eye for the inner form shared by a Culture's art, mathematics, law, and politics. Read the chapter for that eye, whatever you make of the metaphysics around it.

selector.exact: He roots in the earth that he tends

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: with this, man himself becomes plant — namely, as peasant. He roots in the earth that he tends
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: The chapter's first pole: the peasant. With agriculture, 'man himself becomes plant' — rooted, cosmic, blood-and-soil; 'earth becomes Mother Earth,' and the farmhouse is 'the great symbol of settledness,' property 'in the most sacred sense.' Everything Spengler will say against the city is measured against this rootedness. Note that 'blood and soil' here is metaphysics, not yet the slogan the Nazis would later make of it — though the proximity is part of why this volume needs careful reading.

selector.exact: world-history is the history of civic man

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: world-history is the history of civic man
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: The thesis of the whole chapter: 'all great Cultures are town-Cultures,' and 'world-history is the history of civic man.' Peoples, states, religion, the arts and sciences all 'rest upon one prime phenomenon of human being, the town.' This is why Spengler can write a history of cities and call it a history of the world — the city is where a Culture wakes up, builds a soul, and eventually dies.

selector.exact: what distinguishes a town from a village is not size, but the presence of a soul

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: what distinguishes a town from a village is not size, but the presence of a soul
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: A town, for Spengler, is not a big village. It is born when a 'mass-soul of a wholly new kind … suddenly buds off' and the surrounding land is felt, from inside the walls, as 'environs' — 'something different and subordinate.' From that moment 'the man of the land and the man of the city are different essences,' until 'they cease to understand each other at all.' (His example lands sharply: 'a Brandenburg peasant is closer to a Sicilian peasant than he is to a Berliner.')

selector.exact: Money has become, for man as an economic animal, a form of the activity of waking-consciousness, having no longer any roots in Being.

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: Money … a form of the activity of waking-consciousness, having no longer any roots in Being
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: A seed of the volume's last chapters. The city breeds money-thinking, and money, like mathematics, is pure waking-being — abstract, rootless, 'no more limited … by actuality than are the quantities of the mathematical world.' Hence its 'monstrous power over every beginning Civilization,' which is always 'an unconditional dictatorship of money.' Watch the pairing — earth/blood/Being against money/intellect/waking-being — that culminates in the 'blood and money' duel of chapter XIV.

selector.exact: the monstrous symbol and vessel of the completely emancipated intellect, the world-city

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: the world-city … the centre in which the course of a world-history ends by winding itself up
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: The 'world-city' (Weltstadt) is one of Spengler's master-images of decline — Babylon, Alexandria, Baghdad, imperial Rome, and now London, Paris, Berlin, New York. A handful of these giant places reduce 'the entire motherland of its own Culture' to mere 'provinces,' and dissolve every old contrast (noble/commoner, free/slave, believer/unbeliever) into one: 'cosmopolitans and provincials.' The 'stone Colossus, Cosmopolis,' standing at the end of every Culture's life, is the body of its dying intellect.

selector.exact: I see, long after A.D. 2000, cities laid out for ten to twenty million inhabitants

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: long after A.D. 2000, cities laid out for ten to twenty million inhabitants
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: Here (writing in 1922) Spengler foresees cities of 'ten to twenty million inhabitants … with notions of traffic and communication that we should regard as fantastic to the point of madness' — a prediction that, on scale, simply came true. This is the kind of hit Theodor Adorno had in mind when he wrote that 'The forgotten Spengler takes his revenge by threatening to be right.' Adorno's caution is the right frame — such strikes sit beside many misses, and the value is in the diagnostic eye for the megalopolis, not in the determinism that claims to have foreseen it by law.

selector.exact: Primitive folk can loose themselves from the soil and wander, but the intellectual nomad never.

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: Spengler's name for the late-Civilization city-dweller: the 'intellectual nomad,' as homeless in spirit as the primitive hunter was homeless in body, but unable ever to return to the land. 'Homesickness for the great city is keener than any other nostalgia.' The cosmic beat in him fades while the tension of waking-being rises — and that imbalance, he argues, is the inner cause of everything that follows in the chapter, including its bleakest theme.

selector.exact: the sterility of civilized man

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: Read with care. Spengler reads the falling urban birth-rate not as economics but as 'an essentially metaphysical turn towards death': 'When the ordinary thought of a highly cultivated people begins to regard "having children" as a question of pro's and con's, the great turning-point has come.' The demographic fact — fertility decline in late, urban societies — is real and observable (he cites Polybius on Greece, Augustus's marriage laws, Roosevelt's 'race suicide'). But the diagnosis is fused with a reactionary picture of women's emancipation: the 'Ibsen woman' who 'belongs to herself' is treated as a symptom of decline. Take the demographic observation seriously; treat the verdict on women's autonomy as the period's ideology, not a finding.

selector.exact: This residue is the Fellah type.

decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: At the last, only the primitive blood remains … This residue is the Fellah type.
decline-vol-ii-ch-04-cities-and-peoples-.note: The end of the city's history: after the long depopulation, 'the whole pyramid of cultural man vanishes,' crumbling from the world-cities downward, until 'only the primitive blood remains' — the 'Fellah type,' sheltering in the empty stone shells of dead cities 'as the men of the Stone Age sheltered in caves.' The name comes from the Egyptian fellaheen, the peasants who lived on among the ruins of the pharaohs. Spengler develops this post-historical figure fully in chapter VI.

selector.exact: What has inhabited the earth since the Ice Age is man

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: What has inhabited the earth since the Ice Age is man, not 'peoples.'
decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: The chapter sets out 'to demolish' a Romantic idea: 'the People' as a primordial, fixed unit out of which history is made — the 'Nation' of 1789, the 'Volk' of 1813. For Spengler this is a back-projection. The real, separable realities are two — race (the blood, rooted in a landscape) and language (the form of waking-consciousness) — and they have wholly independent histories. 'Peoples' are something later and made, not the bedrock; he untangles them here so he can redefine 'people' and 'nation' in the next chapter.

selector.exact: of Being, is invested with a character of duration. This I call race.

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: the cosmic-plantlike side of life, of Being … This I call race.
decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: Spengler's own definition, and it is crucial for reading the rest of the volume: 'race' is 'the blood which circles, carried on by procreation, in a narrow or a wide landscape' — the duration of the cosmic, Being side of life, bound to a soil. It belongs with time, destiny, and yearning, where language belongs with space and fear. This is a metaphysical, not a biological, concept — as the next notes make emphatic.

selector.exact: Race is something cosmic and psychic

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: Race is something cosmic and psychic … Languages, on the other hand, are causal forms
decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: The structural pair behind the chapter's title 'Peoples, Races, Tongues.' Race and language are two independent realities with two independent histories: race is cosmic and psychic — the Being side, periodic, blood-deep, belonging to time and destiny; language is a 'causal form' — the waking-being side, belonging to space and communication. A man is at once 'derivative of a blood-unit' and 'member of an understanding-unit,' and the two need never coincide. Confusing them, Spengler says, is exactly the error baked into the word 'peoples.'

selector.exact: There are, therefore, expression-languages and communication-languages.

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: A distinction worth carrying out of this chapter. An 'expression-language' merely displays a presence — 'the longing inherent in all life to actualize itself before witnesses' — and assumes only an 'I'; a 'communication-language' is addressed to a 'thou' and needs a shared sense of meanings. Art and religion live mostly in the first, everyday speech in the second. Spengler's lovely corollary: 'it is by some "thou" that we first came to the knowledge of an "I,"' so that the self is born from address — 'a designation for the fact that a bridge exists to some other being.'

selector.exact: Blumenbach classified the races of man according to skull-forms

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: Blumenbach … skull-forms; Müller … hair; Topinard … skin-colour; Huxley …
decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: Spengler skewers the race-science of his day as nationally self-serving guesswork: 'Blumenbach classified the races of man according to skull-forms, Friedrich Müller (as a true German) by hair and language-structure, Topinard (as a true Frenchman) by skin-colour and shape of nose, and Huxley (as a true Englishman) by … sport characteristics' — descriptions 'as worthless as the descriptions of "wanted" men.' The 'soulless causal concatenation of superficial characters' is exactly the materialist method he rejects everywhere; here it happens to land on the anthropometry that the following decade would weaponize.

selector.exact: Comradeship breeds races.

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: The clearest proof that Spengler's 'race' is made, not inherited. A nobility or a war-band, standing 'spiritually firm and united in the presence of its Destiny,' forms a bodily ideal and so 'breeds' a race — 'French noblesse and Prussian Landadel are genuine race-denotations,' and the same shared destiny 'bred the types of the European Jew … with his thousand years of ghetto life.' Race here is forged by comradeship and a 'race-ideal,' independent of any choosing of wives — the opposite of a doctrine of fixed descent.

selector.exact: has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land

decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: 'race' has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land
decline-vol-ii-ch-05-cities-and-peoples-.note: The load-bearing point of the chapter, and one a modern reader must hold firmly. Spengler's 'race' is NOT the biological race of his era's anthropology — the bulk of the chapter demolishes skull-measuring race-science (Blumenbach, Huxley, the very idea of an 'Aryan skull'). 'As soon as light is let through it' — in an X-ray — '"race" vanishes.' For Spengler race is a living quality of flesh, movement, and above all land: bone-structure is 'a growth of the landscape and never a function of the blood,' so 'race has travelled as flesh over the fixed skeleton-form of the land.' This matters historically: when the Nazis later seized his words 'blood' and 'race,' they meant the biological racism Spengler here attacks — he ridiculed Aryan skull-science, quarrelled with the Nazi race-theorist Rosenberg, and saw his 1933 book suppressed by the regime. But do not mistake him for a liberal or a tidy anti-Nazi: he voted for Hitler in 1932, was an authoritarian nationalist, and carried his own civilizational racism (the 'Coloured World-Revolution' he dreaded in The Hour of Decision). His quarrel was with race-biology, not with racial hierarchy as such. So read his 'blood' here as metaphysics (Being, destiny, soil-rootedness) rather than descent — but read the man with both eyes open.

selector.exact: The great events of history were not really achieved by peoples; they themselves created the peoples.

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: the 'people' is a unit of the soul … the great events … created the peoples
decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: Spengler's definition, completing the demolition of chapter V: 'the people is a unit of the soul' — not a race, a language, or a political body, but a community held together by an inner form. And the order of cause runs backward from the usual story: 'the great events of history were not really achieved by peoples; they themselves created the peoples.' It was the migrations that made the Ostrogoths a people; the events came first, the people after. A people is a result, not a precondition.

selector.exact: A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian
decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: A pointed example that 'people' is soul, not blood. In Parthian–Sassanid times 'not tribal attachment but orthodoxy' became 'the hall-mark of nationality': 'A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian; a Persian who became a Christian belonged to the Nestorian "people."' The Persians, Spengler notes, were once 'Aryans, but no people,' then 'a people, but without race.' Nationality here is a matter of belief and inner form — the very opposite of the descent-based 'race' his contemporaries meant.

selector.exact: Out of the people-shapes of the Carolingian Empire

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: Out of the people-shapes of the Carolingian Empire … arise suddenly the German, the French, the Spaniards, the Italians
decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: The decisive reversal. Historians treat the Culture-peoples — German, French, Spanish, Italian — as 'primaries' and the Culture as their product. Spengler turns it around: 'in the tenth century the Faustian soul suddenly awoke,' and out of the older Carolingian tribal shapes (Saxons, Swabians, Franks, Lombards) it created the modern nations. The Culture is the cause; the nation is the form its awakening takes. Peoples are how a Culture appears in the world, not its raw material.

selector.exact: Peoples in the style of their Culture we will call Nations

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: Peoples in the style of their Culture we will call Nations … underlying the nation there is an Idea
decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: Spengler's term for the highest people-form: a 'nation' is a people living 'in the style of' a high Culture, distinguished from the primitive peoples before it and the fellaheen after. What forges it is not merely a feeling of 'we' but 'an Idea' — 'a very deep relation to Destiny, to Time, and to History.' This is why, for him, the modern nation is not eternal but datable: it appears with its Culture and fades with it.

selector.exact: nations of Faustian style emerge, more and more distinctly, from the time of Otto the Great

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.anchor: nations of Faustian style emerge … from the time of Otto the Great
decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: Each Culture, true to Spengler's method, makes nations in its own image, and the three are incommensurable. The Classical nation is a body of citizens bound to a Polis (Apollinian — the present, the body); the Magian/Arabian nation is a community of believers in which 'orthodoxy' defines nationality (the convert changes people); the Faustian nation is a dynamic, idea-bearing people, emerging from about Otto the Great (936–973) and feeling itself German, French, Spanish by A.D. 1000. Three different things wear the one word 'nation.'

selector.exact: the fellah-instincts of Hellenism

decline-vol-ii-ch-06-cities-and-peoples-.note: The chapter's three ages of a people are primitives (before the Culture), nations (the living Culture), and fellaheen (after it). The 'fellaheen' are post-historical peoples — the late Egyptians among the pyramids, the late Chinese, post-Actium Hellenism — whose Culture is spent and who live on without history, swayed by 'fellah-instincts.' The name is borrowed from the Egyptian fellaheen, the peasants who endured among the ruins of the pharaohs. For Spengler the West, too, is bound for this condition; it is the human end-state of his whole morphology.

selector.exact: an older alien Culture lies so massively over the land that a young Culture, born in this land, cannot get its breath

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: historical pseudomorphosis: an older alien Culture lies so massively over the land that a young Culture … cannot get its breath
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: One of Spengler's most influential coinages, borrowed from mineralogy: a 'pseudomorph' is a crystal forced to fill the hollow mould of a different, dissolved mineral, so that its inner structure contradicts its outer shape. A 'historical pseudomorphosis' is a young Culture compelled to grow inside the cast of an older alien one — 'young feelings stiffen in senile works,' and the thwarted soul turns to 'a hate that grows to be monstrous.' The concept has had a long life in cultural history well beyond Spengler.

selector.exact: This is the case of the Arabian Culture.

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: This is the case of the Arabian Culture.
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: The prime case of pseudomorphosis. A young Eastern soul began to wake among the Aramaic-speaking peoples from about 300 B.C. — 'a new relation of man to God' stirring under the names of Ahuramazda, Baal, and Yahweh — but the Macedonian conquest and then Rome laid 'a thin sheet of Classical Civilization' over it. The Magian springtime could not breathe in its own forms; it lived a borrowed, distorted life under Greek and Roman moulds. Everything Spengler says about the Magian soul (next chapter) is meant to recover the true form beneath that cast.

selector.exact: The Magian Culture, geographically and historically, is the midmost of the group of higher Cultures

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: The Magian Culture … is the midmost of the group of higher Cultures
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler's proudest 'discovery': that early Christianity, Judaism and the Talmud, Mazdaism, Mani, Byzantium, and Islam are not separate stories but expressions of one 'Arabian' or 'Magian' Culture. Western scholarship missed it, he argues, because it cut the material along philological and confessional lines — 'Ancient/Mediæval/Modern,' Greek vs. Hebrew vs. Iranian departments — and so 'the big problems' were never even seen. His striking corollary: 'a nation of Magian type has no mother tongue' (its literature is one in spirit but written in many languages).

selector.exact: The Pseudomorphosis began with Actium; there it should have been Antony who won.

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: The Pseudomorphosis began with Actium; there it should have been Antony who won.
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler reads the battle of Actium (31 B.C.) against the grain: not Rome vs. Greece, but 'the unborn Arabian Culture … opposed to iron-grey Classical Civilization' — 'the issue lay between Principate and Caliphate.' Antony's defeat 'drew over its lands the hard sheet of Roman Imperium.' He sharpens it with a counterfactual: had the Arabs won at Tours-Poitiers (732), the West itself would have been the pseudomorphosis — Gothic feeling forced into Mosque and Arabesque, German mysticism replaced by 'a sort of Sufism.'

selector.exact: A second pseudomorphosis is presented to our eyes to-day in Russia.

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: A second pseudomorphosis is presented to our eyes to-day in Russia.
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler applies the concept to the present: Russia is a young Culture forced under a Western, 'Petrine' cast since Peter the Great built Petersburg ('the first act of Antichrist'). Hence the 'apocalyptic hatred' of 'Europe.' His famous epigram: 'Tolstoi is the former Russia, Dostoyevski the coming Russia' — Tolstoi still bound to the West ('the great spokesman of Petrinism even when he is denying it'), Dostoyevski reaching past it to a deeper religiousness. He reads Bolshevism not as Russia's own voice but as 'the final issue of Petrinism,' a fresh pseudomorphosis — a reading worth weighing critically against the history that followed.

selector.exact: What alone matters is the salvation of the soul.

decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: 'Give unto Cæsar …' … What alone matters is the salvation of the soul.
decline-vol-ii-ch-07-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler reads Jesus as belonging wholly to the Magian world of soul and truth, indifferent to the Roman world of fact and power. 'Render unto Caesar' means: 'fit yourselves to the powers of the fact-world, be patient, suffer' — 'what alone matters is the salvation of the soul.' The trial before Pilate becomes the archetypal collision of the two worlds mapped in chapter I: the man of fact ('What is truth?') face to face with the man for whom only truth, never fact, is real.

selector.exact: possesses a kind of extension that may be called cavern-like

decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: the Magian world … a kind of extension that may be called cavern-like
decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.note: The Magian prime-symbol: the world as a cavern. Where the Faustian soul feels space as infinite distance and the Apollinian as a finite body, the Magian feels it as a vaulted, enclosed cavern in which Light and Darkness contend. The dome and the mosque, alchemy and algebra, the dualism of spirit and flesh, the descent of divine substance into the dark 'house of the body' — all, for Spengler, unfold from this one space-feeling. It is the deep form the pseudomorphosis hid.

selector.exact: The world-as-cavern is just as different from the world-as-extent

decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: The world-as-cavern is just as different from the world-as-extent of the … Faustian
decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.note: A compact statement of Spengler's three space-feelings, the heart of his comparative method. The Magian 'world-as-cavern' (enclosed, dual, lit from within) stands against the Faustian 'world-as-extent' (infinite, far-thrusting space) and the Apollinian world-as-body (finite, present, corporeal). 'Space' is not one thing experienced differently but three different things; each Culture's mathematics, architecture, religion, and politics are variations on its own prime-symbol. This is the engine behind the Comparative Tables.

selector.exact: The light-diffusing pearl that illumines the dark house of the body is the Spirit entered into man

decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: the … pearl that illumines the dark house of the body is the Spirit entered into man
decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.note: The Magian world-feeling is dualistic through and through. In the cavern there are two substances — divine Spirit (pneuma) and dark flesh — and salvation is the descent of light into the 'dark house of the body.' From this dualism, Spengler argues, flow Gnosticism, the idea of Grace as a substance, the resurrection of the flesh, and the whole atmosphere of early Christianity, Mani, and the mystery religions. It is a soul utterly unlike the Faustian, whose drama is an infinite striving rather than a rescue of light from darkness.

selector.exact: the Word itself that was sent down as Substance

decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: the Word … that was sent down as Substance
decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.note: A defining Magian idea: the holy Word is not a text about God but a divine Substance 'sent down' — the Koran 'made visible,' the Logos that 'became Flesh.' Because the Word is substance, its very letters carry power: hence the Kabbalah, which 'out of numbers, letter-forms, points, and strokes unfolds secret significances.' Spengler sees one impulse behind the Christian, Jewish, and Islamic scriptural cults — the Word as the medium through which the cavern-world's light reaches man.

selector.exact: Against Arius, who saw in Christ a demigod

decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: Against Arius, who saw in Christ a demigod … Father and Son were of the same substance
decline-vol-ii-ch-08-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler reads the great dogma-battles — Athanasius against Arius, the homoousios ('same substance') of Father and Son — not as theological hairsplitting but as the Magian soul working out the relation of its two substances in the cavern-world. The question 'what of Christ?' is the question of how divine and human substance meet in one figure. The Western formula 'the Word became Flesh,' he insists, already misreads a Magian mystery in the more corporeal terms of the cult-Churches.

selector.exact: These are modes of slipping out of awareness by the aid of being

decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: sleep, wine, dancing … modes of slipping out of awareness by the aid of being
decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler grounds religion in the metaphysics of Volume Two's opening. Waking-being is tension and fear; religion is the longing to escape it back into Being, 'the cosmic, the it' — 'the escape out of space into time.' Sleep, wine ('holy' intoxication), the Dionysian dance are lower modes of this release; faith and worship are the highest. Religiousness, for Spengler, is fundamentally the fear of the invisible and the craving for deliverance from the light-world — not a set of doctrines but a direction of the soul.

selector.exact: Pythagoras, Mohammed, and Cromwell embody one and the same movement in three Cultures.

decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: Pythagoras, Mohammed, and Cromwell embody one and the same movement in three Cultures.
decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.note: The chapter's title-thesis and a bold piece of 'contemporaneity.' Spengler claims Pythagoras (Classical), Mohammed (Magian), and Cromwell (Faustian) occupy the same position in their Cultures' religious life-courses — each a stern, world-shaping reform that fuses faith with a new ethic and a new order. English Puritanism, on this reading, is the Faustian counterpart of the Pythagorean brotherhood and of early Islam. Whether the parallel illuminates or over-reaches is a fair question — but it shows the comparative method at full stretch.

selector.exact: This next phase I call the Second Religiousness.

decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: This next phase I call the Second Religiousness.
decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.note: One of Spengler's most-cited concepts. After a Civilization's rationalism has exhausted itself, religiousness returns — not the living faith of the Culture's springtime but a late, syncretic, consoling piety. The 'Second Religiousness is the necessary counterpart of Cæsarism,' the two together marking the end-stage: the Augustan age in the Classical, the corresponding moment in China, and (Spengler thinks the West is 'still many generations short' of it) our own future. Its modern harbingers are the occultism and Eastern enthusiasms Spengler catalogues later in this very chapter — theosophy, Christian Science, the 'drawing-room Buddhism' of the West — the same revival he points to again in Man and Technics.

selector.exact: proceeds from an unlimited optimism of the reason

decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: every 'Age of Enlightenment' proceeds from an unlimited optimism of the reason … to … scepticism
decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.note: Spengler's morphology of late religion: every 'Age of Enlightenment' — Socratic Athens, Abbassid Baghdad, eighteenth-century London and Paris — swings from 'an unlimited optimism of the reason' to 'an equally unqualified scepticism,' as the megalopolitan intellect criticizes its imaginary world until it reaches 'the form of the form — itself: namely, nothing.' Reason consumes the faith it grew from and leaves a void — which the Second Religiousness then fills. The same arc, he insists, recurs in every Civilization.

selector.exact: In the end Second Religiousness issues in the fellah-religions.

decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.anchor: In the end Second Religiousness issues in the fellah-religions.
decline-vol-ii-ch-09-problems-of-the-ara.note: The final stage. As a Civilization passes wholly into the 'historyless' state of the fellaheen (chapter VI), its religion does too: 'the opposition between cosmopolitan and provincial piety has vanished,' centuries 'pass unimportantly,' and what remains is 'the unalterable finality of the inner state.' The late Egyptian, Chinese, and Islamic worlds are his examples of this changeless fellah-religiousness — and, on his scheme, the destiny toward which the Faustian world is bound.

selector.exact: These lives do not exist in order to be merely lived, but to have meaning.

decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.anchor: the prime Estates … sustained … by a symbolic dignity … lives that exist to have meaning
decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.note: What sets nobility and priesthood apart from peasant and burgher is not power but symbolic dignity — 'a rank derived from a divine investiture and therefore beyond all criticism,' carrying 'the sternest self-discipline … and death itself if need be.' Their lives 'do not exist in order to be merely lived, but to have meaning.' This is the aristocratic-religious world-feeling Spengler measures the modern, leveling 'Third Estate' against — and finds wanting.

selector.exact: Every nobility is a living symbol of Time, every priesthood of Space.

decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.anchor: Every nobility is a living symbol of Time, every priesthood of Space.
decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.note: The metaphysics of Volume Two, now turned into a theory of social class. The two 'prime Estates' embody the two sides of life: the nobility is a symbol of Time, Destiny, race, Being — 'the noble lives in a world of facts,' a doer; the priesthood is a symbol of Space, Causality, intellect, waking-being — 'the priest in one of truths,' a thinker. Aristocracy is 'pulse-sense,' priesthood proceeds 'by tensions.' All Spengler's earlier oppositions (destiny/causality, fact-man/truth-man, blood/intellect) reappear as nobility versus clergy.

selector.exact: The two Estates in principle exclude one another.

decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.anchor: The two Estates in principle exclude one another.
decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.note: Nobility and priesthood are mutually exclusive yet interdependent — 'each is possible and necessary only through the other.' Their opposition is 'the prime opposition of cosmic and microcosmic.' Spengler reads the great medieval struggle of Empire and Papacy, of State and Church, as this estate-opposition raised, in Faustian man, to an intensity no other Culture reached. Hold the pattern: the same dual structure organizes his account of all politics.

selector.exact: Every new Culture has potentially its nobility and its priesthood.

decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.anchor: Every new Culture has potentially its nobility and its priesthood.
decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.note: The two prime estates are universal: Spengler finds them in China, in early Orphism, in the Gothic. Their opposition drives all social history — 'impulsive peasant wars in the Springtime, intellectually-based civil wars in the later period.' The claim is part of his comparative morphology: not just art and mathematics but the very structure of society repeats, Culture to Culture, the same form in different costumes.

selector.exact: The Third Estate, without proper inward unity, was the non-estate

decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.anchor: The Third Estate … the non-estate — the protest … against the existence of estates
decline-vol-ii-ch-10-the-state-the-probl.note: The bourgeoisie is the 'Third Estate' — but really, Spengler says, a 'non-estate': a protest 'against the symbolic view of life in general,' recognizing only 'differences justified by reason or practically useful.' It is the city and the intellect set against the blood-and-meaning of nobility and priesthood. This is the social face of the same money-and-mind 'party' that the philosophy-of-politics chapter (XII) will pit against the old estates.

selector.exact: State is history regarded as at the halt, history the State regarded as on the move.

decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.anchor: State is history regarded as at the halt, history the State regarded as on the move.
decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.note: Spengler's definition of the State, and a neat application of his Being/waking-being method. A people is a being-stream with 'Direction'; look at the movement and you see history, look at the same thing held still — 'the Form as such, as something extended and timelessly standing fast' — and you see the State. They are not two things but one seen two ways: 'State is history regarded as at the halt.' This is why, for him, a State is a living physiognomy, never a paper system.

selector.exact: an existence-unit of history is spoilt when we seriously subject its movement to the constraint of a written constitution

decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.anchor: a people 'in form' … is spoilt when … subjected to … a written constitution
decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.note: A politically loaded idea. A people is 'in form' the way a racehorse or an army is 'in form' — at peak condition for its wrestle with history — and 'only the smallest part of this can be got at by means of the reason.' The living constitution is 'the unwritten, the indescribable, the usual, the felt,' which 'outweigh everything else,' so a people 'is spoilt' when forced under a paper constitution. Read critically: this 'living form over written law' instinct is the same one that fed the anti-parliamentary mood of his moment, and it cuts against the constitutionalism most readers will value.

selector.exact: Culture is the being of nations in State-form.

decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.anchor: Culture is the being of nations in State-form.
decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.note: A nation, Spengler says, possesses a State 'not merely as a condition of movement, but also (above all) as an idea.' And 'the State of the grand style is as old as and no older than its two prime Estates, nobility and priesthood' — it emerges with the Culture and vanishes into it. So the State is not a contract or an institution bolted onto a people; it is what a living Culture looks like when it acts in history.

selector.exact: two such life-forms, Estate and State, contend for supremacy

decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.anchor: Estate and State contend for supremacy — the social against the political
decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.note: Spengler's frame for all internal politics: 'Estate' (the social — nobility, priesthood, later money and mind, each pressing its own interest) versus 'State' (the political — the whole people as one acting unit). The social powers always try 'to subordinate the State-ideal to its own Estate-ideal,' and in the extreme 'the one element succeeds so completely as to make the other its tool.' His sympathies lie with the State as a whole over any class that would capture it — a thread that runs straight into the money-and-democracy critique of chapter XII.

selector.exact: I mean that kind of government which, irrespective of any constitutional formulation that it may have, is in its inward self a return to thorough formlessness

decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.anchor: Cæsarism: government which … is in its inward self a return to thorough formlessness
decline-vol-ii-ch-11-the-state-state-and.note: Spengler's name for the final political form of every Civilization. After money has hollowed out the old constitutional forms, their 'spirit is dead,' and power reverts to sheer personal force — a strong man ruling through institutions that have become empty shells (Spengler's examples: Augustus in Rome, Hwang-ti in China, Amasis in Egypt, Alp Arslan in Baghdad, each behind dead antique forms). Caesarism is, paradoxically, a 'return to formlessness': the end of the age of grand forms and the beginning of a long, post-historical drift. Spengler expected it as the West's near future — the prophecy most argued-over today.

selector.exact: War is the primary politics of everything that lives

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: War is the primary politics of everything that lives
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: The same inversion of Clausewitz as in Man and Technics: politics is sublimated war, not war a tool of politics. The 'race-relation' between peoples is 'for that very reason a relation of war'; even when statesmen learn 'to dispense with war,' the will-to-battle only transfers — 'instead of the conflict of states we have that of parties.' Diplomacy and the war-art share one character; high policy 'tries to be a substitution of more intellectual weapons for the sword,' but never escapes the battle beneath.

selector.exact: The prime Party is that of money and mind, the liberal, the megalopolitan.

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: The prime Estates are nobility and priesthood. The prime Party is that of money and mind, the liberal
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: Spengler maps the whole of politics onto two oppositions. The 'prime Estates' are nobility and priesthood (blood and faith, race and intellect-as-vocation); the 'prime Party' is 'money and mind, the liberal, the megalopolitan.' Aristocracy 'despises the mind of the cities,' Democracy 'despises the boor and hates the countryside.' Estate versus party, growth versus construction, race versus intellect — the late, urban, money-driven 'party' politics is what replaces the old estate-order as a Culture passes into Civilization.

selector.exact: driving every democracy further and further on the road to suicide

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: an irresistible tendency driving every democracy … on the road to suicide
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: Spengler's central political prophecy — mass democracy hollowed out by money and the press, sliding toward Caesarism — is the part of him that a serious reassessment has to weigh rather than wave away. John Farrenkopf's Prophet of Decline reappraises Spengler's whole philosophy of world-history and politics — his geopolitics, his place in the realist tradition, and the deepening pessimism of his later work — and treats his diagnoses of the late, Civilization-phase West, this one included, as claims to engage and argue with rather than dismiss, even where his determinism and his sympathies repel. Read the 'road to suicide' verdict in that spirit: a provocation to test, not a forecast to trust.

attribution.work: Prophet of Decline: Spengler on World History and Politics

selector.exact: on the soil of a democracy constitutional rights signify nothing without money and everything with it

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: on the soil of a democracy constitutional rights signify nothing without money and everything with it
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: The core of Spengler's notorious thesis — often summarized as 'democracy is the political weapon of money' (a paraphrase; the bare slogan is not his exact wording). Under a universal franchise, he argues, formal rights are empty without the means to move the mass — and money supplies the means. His evidence is Rome: 'election-capital rose to American dimensions,' Caesar outspent Pompey and Crassus, and 'Caesar grasped the fact that on the soil of a democracy constitutional rights signify nothing without money and everything with it.' Whatever one makes of the verdict, the mechanism he describes — money converting into political power through elections — is uncomfortably recognizable.

selector.exact: The Press to-day is an army with carefully organized arms and branches, with journalists as officers, and readers as soldiers.

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: The Press … an army … journalists as officers, and readers as soldiers
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: One of the most prescient passages in the book. The press is money's weapon for manufacturing 'public opinion': 'here also money triumphs and forces the free spirits into its service.' The reader is a soldier who 'obeys blindly,' his 'will to think … only a willingness to think to order, and this is what he feels as his liberty.' Written in the age of Northcliffe, it reads as a diagnosis of mass media a century on — a place where Spengler's dark eye for the megalopolis genuinely sees far.

selector.exact: a terrible censorship of silence

decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.anchor: the Press can condemn any 'truth' to death by not communicating it — a censorship of silence
decline-vol-ii-ch-12-the-state-philosoph.note: The sharpest turn of the press argument: its deepest power is not what it prints but what it withholds. The press 'can condemn any truth to death simply by not undertaking its communication to the world — a terrible censorship of silence, … all the more potent in that the masses … are absolutely unaware that it exists.' Spengler contrasts the old, visible censorship of 'stake and faggots' with this new, invisible 'great silence' — an insight that outlives most of his predictions.

selector.exact: in place of thinking in goods we have thinking in money

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: in place of thinking in goods we have thinking in money
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: The hinge of the economics chapters. The land thinks in goods (giving and taking, need); the city thinks in money (prices, sales, profit). With money-thinking the trader stops being 'an organ of economic life' and becomes 'its master,' and the middleman raises 'mediation to a monopoly.' Spengler calls acquisition an 'intellectual plunder' — not a moral charge so much as a description of a wholly new, abstract, city-bred way of experiencing the economy.

selector.exact: Democracy is the completed equating of money with political power.

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Democracy is the completed equating of money with political power.
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: The economic chapter restates the political one. In a money-Civilization 'every idea, to be actualized, has to be put into terms of money': 'at the beginning a man was wealthy because he was powerful — now he is powerful because he has money,' and 'intellect reaches the throne only when money puts it there.' So 'democracy is the completed equating of money with political power' — the same nexus the philosophy-of-politics chapter drew, now seen from the side of the economy.

selector.exact: a desperate conflict waged by the soil-rooted tradition of a race, by its soul, against the spirit of money

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: a desperate conflict … the soil-rooted tradition of a race … against the spirit of money
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: Here the volume's climactic duel is named. Through every Culture's economy 'runs a desperate conflict' — the blood, the soil-rooted soul of a people, against 'the spirit of money,' which 'aims at mobilizing all things' ('he who mobilizes the soil dissolves it into dust'). The early peasant-wars are 'the first reaction of the blood against the money.' Hold this opposition — blood/Being against money/waking-being — for the final chapter, where Spengler stages its last battle.

selector.exact: money, too, therefore represented such a body, in the same way as an Apollo-statue represented a god

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Classical money is a body — the coin, born ~650 B.C. beside the Doric temple and the statue
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: Money, like everything else, takes the shape of its Culture's prime-symbol. The Apollinian soul felt the world as bodies present-at-hand, so its money is the coin — 'a metal weight of beautiful impressed form,' born about 650 B.C. 'simultaneously with the stone body of the Doric temple and the free statue.' Money as magnitude, a body among bodies. 'The coin as money is a purely Classical phenomenon,' Spengler insists — which is why the West only imitates its shape without its meaning.

selector.exact: capable, therefore, of being thought of as money

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: the slave — a body, 'a thing and not a person' — thought of as money
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: A stark consequence of money-as-body. When coin ran short in the late Republic, Rome made money out of the slave — 'another sort of body, but a thing and not a person.' Slaves 'answered their purpose by simply existing, as a stock of money in hand,' which (Spengler argues) explains the otherwise inexplicable slave-masses, the slave-wars, and the Delos market that 'must have dealt with ten thousand slaves a day.' At the sack of Corinth, melting statues into coin and auctioning the inhabitants were 'one and the same operation.' Spengler reports this particular morphology clinically, withholding here the obvious moral judgment (elsewhere in the chapter he is freer with it); the reader should supply what he leaves unsaid.

selector.exact: Double-entry book-keeping is a pure Analysis of the space of values

decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Faustian money is a function — double-entry book-keeping, the book-value; Pythagoras vs Descartes
decline-vol-ii-ch-13-the-form-world-of-e.note: The Faustian soul feels the world as force and function, so its money is not a body but a relation: 'a field of money-tensions' that assigns every object 'a positive or negative effect-value … represented by a book-entry' — 'Quod non est in libris, non est in mundo' ('what is not in the books is not in the world'). Spengler dates this to double-entry book-keeping. 'Here, as ever, Pythagoras and Descartes stand opposed' — money-as-magnitude against money-as-function, the very contrast that divided Classical number from Western number in Volume I.

selector.exact: thrusts itself upon Nature, with the firm resolve to be its master

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Faustian technics … thrusts itself upon Nature, with the firm resolve to be its master
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The volume's last chapter is the seed of Man and Technics (1931). Classical man 'contemplated' Nature like Aristotle's god; the Arabian alchemist sought magical short-cuts; only 'the Western strives to direct the world according to his will.' 'Theory is working hypothesis from the outset' — science as an instrument of power, not contemplation — and the 'passion of the third dimension' (the Faustian craving for infinite space) drives it. Read this chapter and Man and Technics together: the same argument, the longer here and the sharper there.

selector.exact: they forced this secret out of God in order themselves to be God

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: the Gothic discoverers … forced this secret out of God in order themselves to be God
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The Faustian inventor is the Faust-figure: the Gothic monks 'who with prayers and fastings wrung God's secret out of him,' dreaming of perpetual motion, 'created the idea of the machine as a small cosmos obeying the will of man alone' — 'they forced this secret out of God in order themselves to be God.' And with that they crossed the line 'whereat the reverent piety of others saw the beginning of sin'; 'true belief has regarded the machine as of the Devil.' Exactly the Promethean overreach of Man and Technics, here given its Gothic genealogy.

selector.exact: Faustian man has become the slave of his creation.

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Faustian man has become the slave of his creation.
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The reversal at the heart of Man and Technics, stated here too: the master becomes the slave. The machine 'forces the entrepreneur not less than the workman to obedience' — both 'become slaves, and not masters, of the machine.' And it breeds three new figures who eclipse peasant, craftsman, and merchant: the entrepreneur, the engineer, and the factory-worker. Spengler's surprising emphasis falls on the engineer — 'the priest of the machine,' 'the machine's master and destiny' — whose disappearance (if the gifted turn from technics to the soul) would end 'this grand drama' more surely than any strike.

selector.exact: it is a form of thought, it fades out as soon as it has thought its economic world to finality

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: money … is a form of thought; it fades out as soon as it has thought its economic world to finality
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: Spengler's deepest claim about money, and the key to the ending. Money is not a thing but 'a form of thought' (waking-being, like number) — so, unlike a tangible hoard, it is not eternal: once it has 'thought its economic world to finality' and has 'no more material upon which to feed,' it 'fades out.' Having made the machine, the entrepreneur, and the engineer its spoil, money reaches the limit of its own success — and the last conflict opens.

selector.exact: the conflict between money and blood.

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: the last conflict … the conflict between money and blood
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The climax the whole second volume has been building toward. After money's long triumph comes 'the last conflict … in which the Civilization receives its conclusive form — the conflict between money and blood.' Money is waking-being — intellect, the abstract, the world-city; blood is Being — race, soil, the cosmic pulse. Everything Spengler has laid down since chapter I (being/waking-being, destiny/causality, country/city, coin/book-value) converges on this single duel.

selector.exact: a mighty politico-economic order that transcends all class interests

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Spengler's 'Socialism' — an order that transcends all class interests, not Marxism
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: Beware a false friend. When Spengler writes 'Socialism' here he does NOT mean Marx — he treats Marxist labour-politics as a tool of money and democracy. His 'Socialism' is the opposite: 'a mighty politico-economic order that transcends all class interests, a system of lofty thoughtfulness and duty-sense' that keeps the whole 'in fine condition for the decisive battle of its history.' This is the authoritarian, anti-plutocratic — anti-money-power, NOT anti-enterprise: he glorifies the entrepreneur and engineer in this very chapter — duty-and-rulership 'Prussian Socialism' of his 1919 tract Preussentum und Sozialismus, closer to a state-ethic of service than to anything a modern reader means by the word. Do not import the usual meaning.

selector.exact: Money is overthrown and abolished only by blood.

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.anchor: Money is overthrown and abolished only by blood … World-history is the world court
decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The book's resolution, and its hardest passage. Caesarism 'breaks the dictature of money and its political weapon democracy'; 'the sword is victorious over the money, the master-will subdues again the plunderer-will.' 'Money is overthrown and abolished only by blood.' Read with open eyes: this is a frank doctrine of might over right — 'World-history is the world court, and it has ever decided in favour of the stronger … Always it has sacrificed truth and justice to might and race.' It is exactly the blood-over-intellect, force-over-justice conclusion that the authoritarian Right embraced. Spengler offers it as iron description; whether one can read history's verdict as its justification is the deepest question this work leaves you.

selector.exact: Ducunt Fata volentem, nolentem trahunt.

decline-vol-ii-ch-14-the-form-world-of-e.note: The last words of the entire Decline of the West. The Latin is Seneca's (Epistle 107, rendering the Stoic Cleanthes; this closing couplet may be Seneca's own coda): 'The Fates lead the willing; the unwilling they drag.' It is the same ethic Man and Technics ends on — amor fati, the heroic acceptance of a destiny one cannot avert. 'We have not the freedom to reach to this or to that, but the freedom to do the necessary or to do nothing.' Whatever one makes of the politics, the closing note is Stoic resolve in the face of an ending: meet your fate willingly, or be dragged.

selector.exact: first emerges only in the nineteenth century

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: The problem of technics … first emerges only in the nineteenth century
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: Read Man and Technics (1931) as a late, popular distillation of The Decline of the West turned on a single fact: the machine. The same method — Cultures as living organisms with a destiny — is here applied to technics. Spengler's own footnotes keep pointing back to the Decline; treat this short book as that system's coda, not a fresh start.

selector.exact: corrupted only afterward, by Culture

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: a kind of little lamb … corrupted only afterward, by Culture
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: Spengler's target is the Enlightenment picture of natural man — above all Rousseau's, for whom humans were good in the state of nature and corrupted by civilization ('nothing is so gentle as man in his primitive state'). Spengler will invert it completely: man is a beast of prey from the start. He returns to 'the plebeian shadow of Rousseau' in the next chapter.

selector.exact: the doctrine of Bentham, Mill and Spencer

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: the doctrine of Bentham, Mill and Spencer
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: The 'greatest happiness of the greatest number' is the utilitarian principle of Jeremy Bentham and J. S. Mill (Herbert Spencer gave it an evolutionary turn). Spengler caricatures it as a creed of 'not-doing' — comfort and labour-saving raised to an ideal — and reads the dream of an 'Earthly Paradise' of gadgets as the spiritual poverty of a late Civilization.

selector.exact: panem et circenses of the late world-cities

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: the panem et circenses of the late world-cities
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: 'Bread and circuses' — the satirist Juvenal's phrase for the dole-and-games that kept the Roman mob content. Spengler treats modern mass-comfort as the contemporary form of the same late-city phenomenon, the 'world-city' of a Civilization past its creative spring. The Roman parallel is doing real argumentative work, not just decorating the prose.

selector.exact: technics is immemorially old

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: The book's founding redefinition. 'Technics' for Spengler is not machinery, nor even specifically human: it is the tactics of all free-moving life. So a lion stalking a gazelle, or diplomacy, or a brush-stroke, is 'technics'. Hold onto this wide sense — the whole argument depends on technics being something a creature does, not a thing it makes.

selector.exact: of horsemanship, of steering an airship

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: a technics of the brush-stroke, of horsemanship, of steering an airship
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: Spengler's second corrective: technics is not the tool but the purposive activity. 'What matters is not the making of things but the procedure with them; not the weapon, but the battle.' Many techniques use no implement at all. This is why he can build a whole philosophy of technology without ever starting from the machine.

selector.exact: a long life or a short one full of deeds and fame

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: the choice of whether he would wish a long life or a short one full of deeds and fame
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: Achilles' choice, from the Iliad: a long obscure life, or a short one full of glory. Spengler plants it here and harvests it on the book's last page as 'the Choice of Achilles' — the only world-outlook he thinks 'worthy of us'. Notice how the opening and the close are wired together: the heroic-pessimist ethic frames everything in between.

selector.exact: the hubris of Prometheus, who reaches into the heavens

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: the hubris of Prometheus, who reaches into the heavens to subject the divine powers to man
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: The Promethean image — the overreaching hand that steals from the gods and is punished — is the master-metaphor of the whole book. It returns as the human hand (ch. III), the theft of fire, and finally the machine seized 'as when Prometheus stole fire' (ch. V). Spengler's man is Prometheus: his grandeur and his doom are the same act.

selector.exact: going down with our eyes open

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: we men of the twentieth century are going down with our eyes open
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: A key to Spengler's tone. He claims that clear historical sight comes only to a Culture in its decline ('only on the summit of high Cultures … does this gift of penetrating insight appear for a moment'). So the pessimism is not despair but a claimed privilege of vision: we can read the form of our age precisely because we are late in it. Whether that is insight or a self-flattering alibi is a fair question to keep open.

selector.exact: in Nietzsche’s sense, as a struggle out of the will-to-power

man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.anchor: life … in Nietzsche’s sense, as a struggle out of the will-to-power, cruel, inexorable
man-technics-ch-01-technics-as-the-tacti.note: Spengler names his frame openly: life as Nietzsche's 'will to power' (Wille zur Macht). Nietzsche is one of his two masters (with Goethe). The borrowing is selective — Spengler keeps Nietzsche's combative vitalism and 'amor fati' but drops the hope of an 'overcoming'; what is left is a tragic, no-exit struggle.

selector.exact: Ideals are cowardices.

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: A characteristic Spenglerian hand-grenade. The 'ideal', he charges, is a flinching away from a truth one already knows — here, the predatory truth about man. Watch the formula: it is the twin of the book's last line, 'Optimism is cowardice.' Both equate consolation with weakness; both are dares more than arguments.

selector.exact: It gives the type man a high rank, that he is a beast of prey.

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: It gives the type man a high rank, that he is a beast of prey.
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: The central anthropology of the book, stated flat. Spengler ranks life: plant (lived, not living) below herbivore below carnivore, the 'highest form of mobile life'. Man belongs to the predators, and Spengler means this as praise — freedom, solitude, command. Everything about human technics and Culture is derived from this one premise, so it is worth deciding early how much of it you grant.

selector.exact: a world-around for each single being

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: there arise out of the surrounding a world-around for each single being
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: Spengler's 'world-around' is Jakob von Uexküll's Umwelt — the idea that each species builds its own self-centred perceptual world out of what it can sense and act on, so that animals sharing one habitat inhabit different worlds. The term 'Umwelt' is Uexküll's; Spengler adopts it (in the 1931 original he uses the word without a footnote). He uses it to contrast the herbivore's nose-world of flight with the predator's eye-world of command.

selector.exact: The world-picture is the world-around mastered by the eye.

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: The world-picture is the world-around mastered by the eye.
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: The predator's forward-set, binocular, fixing eye turns surroundings into a 'picture' — distance, perspective, objects at definite points — and to see so is already to command. Spengler derives theoretical seeing (cause and effect, 'truth') from the hunting gaze. This is the same move as the Decline's 'Faustian' soul, whose prime-symbol is infinite space: the will to grasp distance.

selector.exact: the kingly concept of property has its root

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: Property, for Spengler, is not a legal right to have but the predator's domain of unlimited power — won in battle, defended against equals, 'the sovereign right to do as one will with one's own'. The economic categories he will use later (booty, enterprise, money) are all rooted back here, in the beast that tolerates no equal in its den.

selector.exact: so thought Nietzsche, amor fati

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: Spengler sets his reading of life-as-struggle against two others he names: not Schopenhauer's 'misery', not Darwin's neutral 'struggle for existence', but Nietzsche's amor fati — the love of one's fate, the willing of necessity as if one had chosen it. It is the affirmative half of the will-to-power, and the ethical seed of the book's stoic ending.

selector.exact: the tactic of his life is that of a splendid, brave, cunning, cruel beast of prey

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: the tactic of his life is that of a splendid, brave, cunning, cruel beast of prey
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: Spengler clears two rivals out of the way at once: Rousseau's gentle natural man and the Darwinians' 'semi-ape with technical tendencies' (he names Haeckel). Man is neither a fallen innocent nor a clever monkey but a predator with a soul. Note that he accepts evolution's facts while rejecting its tone — the quarrel is over meaning, not data.

selector.exact: Ants know plant-culture, road-building, slavery and the conduct of war.

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: Ants know plant-culture, road-building, slavery and the conduct of war.
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: A deliberately humbling list: bees, termites, beavers, ants already 'do' agriculture, building, slavery, war. 'Man achieves nothing that is not achievable by life as a whole.' The difference is not the deed but its source — animal technique is generic and unalterable ('instinct'), while man's is invented anew by each individual. Keep the distinction in view; the next chapter builds the origin of man on it.

selector.exact: Man has become the creator of his tactic of living. That is his greatness and his doom.

man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.anchor: Man has become the creator of his tactic of living. That is his greatness and his doom.
man-technics-ch-02-herbivores-and-beasts.note: The hinge of the whole book. Animal technique is fixed by the species ('the compulsion of the genus') — a bee builds one comb forever. Human technics alone is conscious, inventive, personal: the individual frees himself from the species. That liberation is Spengler's definition of man — and, because it sets him at war with Nature, it is in the same breath his doom. Grandeur and doom are not two facts but one.

selector.exact: The answer runs: through the arising of the hand.

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: The answer runs: through the arising of the hand.
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: Spengler's answer to 'how did man become man?' is: the hand. He calls it 'a weapon unparalleled' — at once the organ of touch and of action, theoretically grasping the world (with the eye) and practically commanding it. The chapter's whole case is that the human story is the story of this one member; everything else (upright gait, the head, the soul) follows from it.

selector.exact: World-history strides forward from catastrophe to catastrophe

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: World-history strides forward from catastrophe to catastrophe
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: A deliberate rejection of nineteenth-century gradualism. Against Lyell's slow geological 'evolution' ('truly appropriate to the English nature, but … not Nature'), Spengler wants sudden, total change, and recruits Hugo de Vries's mutation theory as his scientific ally: man, hand and all, appears 'as abruptly as a flash of lightning'. Note the method — he picks the biology that fits a morphology of leaps, not lines.

selector.exact: As the tool took form from the shape of the hand, so conversely the hand on the shape of the tool.

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: As the tool took form from the shape of the hand, so conversely the hand on the shape of the tool.
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: The title's claim: hand and tool are inseparable and equally old — 'It is meaningless to attempt to divide the two chronologically.' The unarmed hand is 'useless'; it needs a weapon to become a weapon. This co-origination is the template Spengler reuses in ch. IV, where speech and enterprise stand to each other 'as the older pair hand and implement'.

selector.exact: the truth-men — the priest, the scholar, the philosopher — and of the fact-men — the politician, the general, the merchant

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: the truth-men — the priest, the scholar, the philosopher — and of the fact-men — the politician, the general, the merchant
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: From the 'thought of the eye' (contemplation, cause and effect, true/false) and the 'thought of the hand' (action, means and end, suitable/unsuitable) Spengler derives two permanent human types: truth-men (priest, scholar, philosopher) and fact-men (statesman, general, merchant). The pair runs through the rest of the book and through the Decline's politics; an 'aim is a fact, a connexion of cause and effect is a truth.'

selector.exact: Nothing is so telling for the difference as the kindling of fire.

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: Nothing is so telling for the difference as the kindling of fire.
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: Fire is Spengler's example of the first true 'deed' (as against mere animal activity): animals see fire start, but man alone thinks out how to make it — 'one of the most uncanny, violent, enigmatic phenomena of Nature … henceforth called into life by Man himself, against Nature.' The Promethean theme of ch. I is now literal, and the phrase 'against Nature' begins to toll.

selector.exact: Every technical procedure of man is an art and has always been so called

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: Every technical procedure of man is an art and has always been so called
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: Spengler stretches 'Art' to mean everything man-made and so unnatural — archery, war, government, sacrifice, painting, experiment alike. 'Free will itself is an act of rebellion.' This is why, for him, technics and the fine arts are one family: both are the privilege of creation 'wrested from Nature', and every creation pushes man further from her.

selector.exact: for Nature is the stronger. Man remains dependent upon her

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: The tragedy of man begins, for Nature is the stronger
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: The tragic core of the book, stated as plainly as Spengler ever states anything. Man rebels against Nature — 'raises his hand against his mother' — but Nature is the stronger and still 'embraces him … within herself'. The rebellion is therefore doomed in advance and fought anyway. Everything from here, including the machine, is this single tragedy enlarging.

selector.exact: All the great Cultures are so many defeats.

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: All the great Cultures are so many defeats.
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: A line worth pausing on. In the Decline a Culture's life-cycle ends in Civilization and decline; here Spengler radicalizes it — the creative act is itself already a defeat, because it deepens the doomed quarrel with Nature. 'Whole races remain, inwardly destroyed and broken … as corpses on the field.' The grandeur and the catastrophe are, again, the same event.

selector.exact: The struggle against Nature is hopeless, and nevertheless it will be fought out to the end.

man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.anchor: The struggle against Nature is hopeless, and nevertheless it will be fought out to the end.
man-technics-ch-03-the-origin-of-man-han.note: The chapter ends on the ethic the whole book will end on: the fight is hopeless 'and yet … will be fought out to the bitter end.' This is the heroic-pessimist stance — value lies in how one meets a doom one cannot avert, not in any prospect of winning. Hold it next to the Pompeii sentry of the final page; they are the same thought.

selector.exact: at about the fifth millennium B.C.

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: This mutation can be fixed fairly exactly, at about the fifth millennium B.C.
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: Spengler dates a 'second mutation' — pottery, herding, tillage, building, travel — to about 5000 B.C., roughly what archaeology calls the Neolithic revolution, and notes the high Cultures of Egypt and Mesopotamia follow only ~2,000 years later. Notice the accelerating tempo he keeps insisting on: 'with tearing leaps, the rolling stone is approaching the abyss' — geological time gives way to historical time gives way to the crisis of our own century.

selector.exact: planful doing in numbers. Until then each man lives his own life

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: planful doing in numbers. Until then each man lives his own life
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: The spiritual change of the second stage, in three words: 'collective doing by plan' — what Spengler also calls the enterprise. Where each predator-man once made his own weapon and needed no one, now long, multi-person tasks (felling a tree, then years later sailing the ship built from it) demand coordination. And coordination demands a medium — which is why speech, in his argument, must be born at exactly this moment, not before.

selector.exact: Ever since Hamann and Herder, men have again and again set themselves the question of its origin.

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: Ever since Hamann and Herder, men have again and again set themselves the question of its origin.
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: Spengler enters an old German debate on the origin of language (J. G. Hamann, J. G. Herder) only to reject both sides. Against the Romantics (Herder), language did not begin as 'primary poesy' — myth and lyric; against the rationalists, the sentence is not first a 'judgment' or 'thought'. His own answer: speech is practical and dialogic from the start — command, question, answer ('Do this!' 'Ready?' 'Yes!'), the verbal side of getting a job done together.

selector.exact: Speaking and enterprise presuppose each other in precisely the same way as, earlier, hand and tool.

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: Speaking and enterprise presuppose each other in precisely the same way as, earlier, hand and tool.
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: Spengler builds the second stage by analogy to the first: speech is to enterprise what the hand was to the tool — inseparable, co-evolved, two faces of one act. This parallelism is his compositional signature. Spotting it tells you what he thinks language is for: not contemplation but the running of joint undertakings.

selector.exact: as Mephistopheles ironically expresses it in Faust

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: as Mephistopheles ironically expresses it in Faust
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: Spengler illustrates the 'will-to-more-power' of collective enterprise with Goethe's Faust — Mephistopheles' boast that money harnessing six horses makes their twenty-four legs his own. Goethe is the master Spengler credits with his method, and Faust is his recurring emblem of the Western, 'Faustian' soul. The quotation is the Anster translation, as the translator's footnote records.

selector.exact: there are by nature commanders and obeyers

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: there are by nature commanders and obeyers, subjects and objects of the political or economic procedures
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: The enterprise splits not only work (planning vs. executing) but mankind: leaders and led, 'subjects and objects of the political or economic process'. Spengler presents this as a natural fact 'only to be eliminated along with life itself', and ties genius (creative power) to command. This is the anti-egalitarian spine of his politics — track it into the next note, and into the Decline's account of Caesarism.

selector.exact: proves that there is here something to be explained away

man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.anchor: the talk of the ‘natural equality of all’ proves that there is here something to be explained away
man-technics-ch-04-the-second-stage-spee.note: Difficult material, read it with a cold eye. Spengler treats a natural ranking of men as plain fact and the claim that 'all men are equal' as a decadent evasion. This is the elitist, anti-democratic strain he shares with the late-Weimar radical Right, and it is asserted, not demonstrated — the 'fact' does the work a value-judgment should. Later readers generally take this as the ideology of the system, not a finding of it; weigh it as such rather than swallowing or simply skipping it.
